Beside this central icon sits a smaller, similarly adorned figure, both set against a deep red, ritualistically stained wall within the sacred, dimly lit sanctum of the historic temple.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Bhurtī group of three śikharas (13th-15th cent.)
captions: This view of the central stone śikhara, part of a 13th-to-15th-century temple triad, reveals a weathered, tiered tower structure topped with an amalaka. The masonry displays signs of age and lichen growth, flanked by two similar structures in a verdant, hilly landscape, while a human figure provides scale against the massive, block-hewn architectural form.
A stone shrine at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Bhurtī group of three śikharas (13th-15th cent.)
captions: This view of a weathered stone śikhara temple reveals its robust, tiered masonry and curvilinear superstructure, characteristic of medieval Himalayan architecture. The structure features a recessed, dark entrance leading to a sacred interior, while the exterior displays eroded decorative moldings and block-like projections, standing in a serene, grassy landscape that highlights the temple’s enduring, austere form.
A stone liṅga,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southwestern angle, this single-story Calākhu Phalcā features traditional red brick masonry accented by intricately carved dark wooden windows and ornate, weathered struts supporting a modern corrugated tin roof. The structure’s horizontal form, characteristic of a public rest house, showcases the elegant craftsmanship of Newari architecture, where the contrast between the rustic brickwork and the delicate, dark-toned timber detailing highlights the enduring cultural significance of this heritage site.
A 1-storey buddhist,hindu phalcā in nepal.

This northwestern view of the Ajayamerukoṭa Fortress ruins reveals the weathered remnants of stone masonry, characterized by dry-laid, rectangular blocks forming the base of a collapsed structure. Amidst the encroaching vegetation and scattered rubble, a prominent, upright monolithic slab stands as a silent sentinel, evoking the structural austerity of this 14th-century site, while the surrounding remnants hint at the foundational layout of a once-imposing defensive complex atop the rocky spur.
A fortress,archaeological object(s) in nepal.

The northern side of the Jhilṭuṅa Koṭaghara reveals the dilapidated remnants of a historic fortress, where only the subterranean foundations and fragmented brickwork remain amidst the encroaching wild vegetation. This elevated site, once a strategic defensive stronghold, now displays exposed earthworks and scattered masonry, with the original structural layout—likely featuring traditional timber-framed elements or a robust mandapa-style base—completely lost to time and erosion, leaving only a rugged, hollowed-out landscape.
A fortress in nepal.

This humble *daśain ghara*, a traditional ritual structure, is constructed from dry-laid, irregular fieldstones that harmonize with the rugged, rural landscape. The rectangular edifice features a simple corrugated metal roof, a modest barred window, and a vibrant, modern blue-painted door, serving as a secluded, functional site for essential cultural observances held within this historic fortress setting.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

Standing amidst a rural landscape, this weathered wooden *yūpa*, or sacrificial post, features a robust, rectangular base topped by a series of five stacked, inverted-pyramidal tiers. The weathered timber, marked by subtle traces of vermilion, serves as a ritual marker near a rustic stone structure, embodying a traditional form of votive or ceremonial architecture deeply rooted in local cultural practices.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Santāneśvara Mahādeva Mandira in Indrachowk highlights its ornate entrance, framed by a dark stone archway and flanked by golden-painted pillars adorned with intricate relief carvings. Above the door, a striking gilded *toraṇa* displays traditional Newari iconography, while the threshold is scattered with ritual offerings, including marigolds and a citrus fruit, reflecting the active devotional life surrounding this sacred Shaiva shrine dedicated to the Śivaliṅga.
A 2-storey shaiva śikhara temple in nepal.

A gilded, recumbent Nandi, the sacred mount of Shiva, rests atop a dark stone plinth, its stylized form featuring a prominent hump and beaded collar. Adjacent to this devotional sculpture, a pigeon perches upon a small terracotta offering vessel, set against the bustling, out-of-focus backdrop of a traditional urban square in Kathmandu.
A other statue at a nepali heritage site.

A stone śivaliṅga, adorned with a metallic face mask and a cobra canopy, rests upon a circular pīṭha within a rugged, stone-walled sanctum. A copper vessel hangs directly above for ritual ablution, while a small brass bell suspended from the ceiling and scattered floral offerings complete this austere, meditative space dedicated to the worship of Lord Shiva.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Agnimaṭha in Thabu showcases the exquisite craftsmanship of Newari architecture, featuring a red brick facade adorned with intricate dark wood carvings. The central entrance is crowned by a finely sculpted, semi-circular *toraṇa* above the lattice-screened door, while the surrounding walls exhibit traditional window frames and decorative woodwork, reflecting the refined aesthetic of this historic Hindu shrine and its enduring cultural significance in the Kathmandu Valley.
A 2-storey hindu shrine in nepal.

A weathered, recessed brick niche serves as a simple shrine, framed by moss-covered masonry that reflects centuries of exposure. A metal stand holding two small ritual bells stands before the opening, where traces of red sindoor pigment remain on the interior wall, marking this humble, ancient space as a site of ongoing devotional practice within the historic Agnimaṭha complex.
A brick shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Set within a weathered brick courtyard, this humble shrine features three aniconic stones, marked with vermilion, representing Gaṇeśa, Siddhilakṣmī, and Kumārī. These sacred stones are framed by a simple, low-walled stone structure, positioned before a dark, intricately carved wooden tympanum adorned with auspicious symbols, while a small metal bell hangs from a nearby iron stand, marking the site’s ancient devotional significance.
A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

This western-facing view captures a cluster of stone *caityas* standing on a dusty, unpaved ground, characterized by their tiered bases, square *harmikā* sections, and weathered *gajura* pinnacles. These Buddhist monuments, constructed from carved stone, are surrounded by piles of discarded masonry and debris, set against a stark corrugated metal fence that obscures the nearby urban landscape, highlighting the ongoing preservation challenges faced by this historic site.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

Captured from a frontal perspective, this stone icon of Akṣyobhya Buddha is enshrined within a simple, white-plastered niche at Iku Bāhāḥ. The deity sits in a meditative posture upon a double-layered lotus pedestal, known as a *padmāsana*, featuring intricately carved, rounded petals. Traces of vermilion powder highlight the figure, which is set against a pointed, arched stele, reflecting the modest yet sacred aesthetic of this historic Newari monastic courtyard.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone Dharmadhātu, situated within the courtyard of a private Newar monastery, features an octagonal base adorned with intricate relief panels depicting seated deities within arched niches. The structure is crowned by a lotus-petal pedestal encircled by a serpent motif, showcasing the refined craftsmanship characteristic of traditional Newari devotional art in a weathered, historic setting.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a western perspective, this scene features a row of four stone caityas aligned north-south, each crowned with a tiered pinnacle or gajura. These Buddhist monuments, characterized by their square bases and carved niches, stand amidst scattered stone debris and construction materials. Behind them, a corrugated metal fence obscures the surrounding urban landscape, contrasting the weathered, traditional stone masonry with modern, utilitarian barriers in this historic Newari architectural setting.
A buddhist heritage monument in nepal.

A frontal view of a stone statue of Avalokiteśvara, housed within the monastic courtyard of Bhīmarāja Bāhāḥ, stands upon a lotus-carved pedestal adorned with ritual offerings of rice and flowers. The deity is framed by traditional stone pillars and iron bars, characteristic of the site’s monastic architecture, while the figure’s intricate headpiece and serene posture reflect the refined craftsmanship typical of Newari Buddhist iconography found within this historic branch of Oku Bāhāḥ.
A buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

Captured in a frontal view, this dark, weathered stone image of Akṣobhya Buddha sits in the *bhūmisparśa mudrā* upon a classic lotus-petal pedestal within the Devajyoti Bāhāḥ. The deity, adorned with traces of ritual vermilion powder, is flanked by a smaller, indistinct votive stone sculpture, both resting against a rough, textured wall stained with vibrant pigments, reflecting the devotional atmosphere characteristic of this historic Newari Buddhist monastic site.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone *caitya* stands within a traditional Newari courtyard, featuring a tiered pedestal and a prominent central *niche* housing a seated deity. The votive stupa is adorned with intricate lotus petal carvings and topped by a golden finial, while a metal railing with oil lamp holders surrounds the base, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices of the monastic community.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This stone Dharmadhātu maṇḍala, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a central octagonal pedestal topped by a lotus-shaped vessel, all protected by a simple iron frame. The weathered dark stone displays delicate relief carvings along its base and upper rim, reflecting the traditional craftsmanship found in Newari Buddhist architecture, with scattered flower offerings marking its ongoing devotional use.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This high-angle shot captures a *capĩgāḥ*, a traditional sunken water vessel found in Newari settlements, constructed from weathered red bricks and finished with a vibrant blue interior. The structure features a low, oval-shaped masonry rim adorned with subtle relief carvings, while the surrounding dusty ground is scattered with ritual offerings and oil lamps, reflecting its enduring role as a site of communal water worship and historical significance in Sunāguthi.
A hindu,buddhist water architecture in nepal.

This frontal view captures the courtyard of Dune Bāhāḥ, a traditional Newari Buddhist monastic building featuring a weathered, two-storey façade with a gable roof and intricate wooden struts. Centered in the foreground stands a stone *caitya*—a miniature stupa—sheltered by a simple metal-framed canopy, while the building’s entrance is framed by a traditional wooden doorway. The scene blends sacred heritage with modern utility, juxtaposed against contemporary water tanks and a parked motorcycle.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A dark, weathered stone statue of Akshobhya Buddha sits in the *bhumisparsha mudra* (earth-touching gesture) upon a tiered lotus pedestal. The figure wears an ornate, crown-like headpiece adorned with marigold offerings, set against a simple, yellow-painted stone niche that mimics the form of a *toraṇa* within the quiet, austere interior of the monastic *bāhāḥ*.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone Śikharakūṭa caitya, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a multi-tiered structure crowned by a golden finial. The base displays an intricate relief panel depicting a deity within a cusped toraṇa, while a smaller niche above houses a seated figure. Weathered yet preserved, the votive stupa stands framed by metal supports, reflecting the rich architectural heritage of Newari Buddhist traditions.
A stone śikharakūṭa caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from the northern side, this frontal view showcases a weathered, rectangular stone shrine dedicated to Mahākāla, featuring a simple, unadorned monolith topped by a metallic, canopy-like structure supported by slender iron poles. Behind the shrine stands a traditional Newari *pati* or *mandapa* with a tiled, sloped roof, while the surrounding area reflects the rustic, evolving urban landscape of Sunaguthi, a site integral to local religious processions and rituals.
A 1-storey hindu-buddhist pillar,shrine in nepal.

This dark stone relief panel depicts the wrathful deity Mahākāla standing in a dynamic posture, holding a ritual club while adorned with traditional jewelry. Set within a recessed niche, the icon is layered with vermilion paste and offerings of fresh hibiscus flowers, reflecting its active role as a revered object of daily devotion in the local religious landscape.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered, vertical wooden post serves as a support for a ritual brass bell, suspended by a simple iron chain near a stone shrine. The post features an incised relief panel displaying Devanagari script, while the bell exhibits traditional Newari craftsmanship, characterized by its ornate, tiered handle and aged patina, reflecting the devotional atmosphere of this sacred site.
A wood bell at a nepali heritage site.

This elevated, eastern-facing view captures a finely carved *pañcāvaliyukta caitya* situated within a traditional Newari courtyard, framed by a low stone railing. The stone monument features a tiered, lotus-petal base and a central niche housing a small image of Akṣobhya Buddha, all topped by a slender *gajura* adorned with a white cloth. The surrounding weathered brick walls and rustic architectural textures highlight the sacred, intimate character of this historic Buddhist site.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This stone votive shrine, situated within a traditional Newari courtyard, features a central arched niche containing a seated Buddha image, likely Akṣobhya, marked by red vermilion. The structure rests upon a sturdy, brick-faced plinth flanked by two small stone guardian lions, reflecting the modest yet devotional architectural style common in Kathmandu Valley Buddhist bahals.
A stone statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche of a brick-built shrine, depicts the Hindu goddess Sarasvatī holding her characteristic vīṇā. The deity is rendered in a traditional frontal pose, with traces of vermilion powder applied during ritual worship, contrasting against the dark, weathered stone surface framed by an arched, decorative molding reminiscent of a miniature toraṇa.
A stone statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southwestern angle, this compact, single-storey Buddhist shrine within Vachẽ Nani Bāhāḥcā features a traditional brick base and a finely carved wooden sanctum. The structure is crowned by a distinctive white, bell-shaped dome topped with a metallic pinnacle, or gajura, reflecting Newari architectural styles. Nestled between a modern residential building and an aged brick wall, the shrine serves as a quiet, sacred focal point within the dense urban courtyard.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of the deity Gaṇeśa is set within a recessed, dark niche, characterized by its iconic elephant head, prominent trunk, and traditional seated posture. The sculpture, adorned with traces of ritual red pigment, exhibits signs of age and moss accumulation, reflecting its long-standing devotional role within the architectural fabric of this historic Newar Buddhist monastic courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone *śikharakūṭa* caitya, situated within a traditional Newari courtyard, features a tiered, temple-like structure crowned by a miniature *gajura*. The central *caitya* body displays a recessed niche containing a deity relief, while the surrounding stone railing is adorned with small, ritualistic oil lamp holders, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices within this historic monastic complex.
A stone śikharakūṭa caitya at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Nārāyaṇa Mandira at Cāka Bāhāḥ (ca. 1699 CE)
captions: This frontal view captures the single-storey Nārāyaṇa Mandira at Cāka Bāhāḥ, a distinct Vaishnava shrine characterized by its brick masonry and a prominent, ornate domed roof topped with a gilded gajura. The structure features a finely carved stone toraṇa above its central doorway, while the surrounding urban landscape, cluttered with overhead power lines and unfinished concrete buildings, highlights the juxtaposition of historic Newari architecture within a rapidly modernizing Patan streetscape.
A 1-storey vaishnava domed temple in nepal.

monument_name: Nārāyaṇa Mandira at Cāka Bāhāḥ (ca. 1699 CE)
captions: A weathered stone Garuda, the avian mount of Lord Vishnu, kneels in anjali mudra atop a tiered, brick-and-mortar pedestal featuring carved relief faces and a long inscribed donor list. Situated within a historic Newari courtyard, this devotional pillar serves as a traditional dhvajastambha, reflecting the intricate craftsmanship and enduring Vaishnava traditions characteristic of the Kathmandu Valley’s architectural heritage.
A stone pillar at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Mahādyaḥ Degaḥ (built around seventeenth and eighteenth century)
captions: A weathered stone sculpture of Nandi, the sacred bull vahana of Shiva, rests in a recumbent posture upon a low brick and mortar plinth within the temple complex. The icon, marked by traces of vermilion and a small floral offering, exhibits significant erosion, yet retains the distinct hump and anatomical features characteristic of traditional Newari devotional stone carvings.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Mahādyaḥ Degaḥ (built around seventeenth and eighteenth century)
captions: This stone *ekamukha-liṅga* features a finely carved relief of a deity in *añjalimudrā*, set upon a circular *yoni-pīṭha* base within a rustic brick alcove. The weathered sculpture, adorned with scattered floral offerings and rice grains, exemplifies the devotional simplicity found in the Bāghabhairava complex, where such sacred icons remain integral to the daily ritual life of the local community.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the historic Naḥ Bāhāḥcā in Lalitpur showcases exquisite Newari craftsmanship, featuring a central wooden doorway adorned with intricate lattice work and a small, carved toraṇa above the lintel. The facade, constructed from traditional red brick, is flanked by two symmetrical windows and crowned by a decorative cornice featuring a series of carved wooden heads, reflecting the sophisticated aesthetic of this ancient Buddhist monastic building.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A stone relief sculpture of a deity, likely Mahākāla, stands within a recessed niche, its dark surface heavily coated in layers of ritual vermilion powder and yellow pigment. The figure is adorned with dried floral offerings, while its robust, stylized form and traditional iconography reflect the enduring devotional practices and artistic legacy characteristic of Newari Buddhist monastic shrines.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief ensemble features a devotional donor figure in a seated, prayerful *añjali mudrā* posture, positioned beneath a prominent, petal-carved canopy. Beside this figure stands a weathered, rounded *caitya* marked by ritual vermilion pigment, both resting against a plain wall within the monastic courtyard, reflecting the enduring tradition of votive offerings in Newari Buddhist practice.
A stone caitya,statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the eastern facade of the Buramāṃ Vaidya Bāhāḥ showcases the exquisite craftsmanship of Newari woodcarving, featuring two tiers of intricately decorated windows framed by elaborate pilasters and horizontal friezes. The historic Buddhist monastic building displays traditional dark timber construction, with two pigeons perched upon the central projecting sill, while the weathered masonry base and structural beams highlight the aged, authentic texture of this 1866 CE heritage site.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone *caitya* stands in a monastic courtyard, featuring a central *dharmadhātu* configuration with four seated Buddhas facing the cardinal directions atop a lotus pedestal. The votive stupa rests on a tiered base adorned with relief carvings and corner lions, set against a backdrop of intricate, white-painted metal lattice screens typical of Newari architectural courtyards.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from the southern side, this 16th-century Taḥgaḥcībhāḥ Caitya stands as a weathered, single-storey Buddhist monument constructed of traditional red brick and stone. The structure features a stepped plinth supporting a central dome crowned by a tiered pinnacle, or gajura, while a small, recessed niche serves as a shrine. Set within the historic urban fabric of Macchindrabāhāḥ, the stūpa is flanked by aged residential buildings and remnants of surrounding architectural heritage.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

Set within a recessed niche of a 16th-century stone caitya, this weathered relief panel depicts the Buddha Akṣobhya in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture. The figure, carved from dark stone and marked with ritual vermilion powder, exhibits significant erosion, yet retains the iconic posture characteristic of the five Tathāgatas typically enshrined in the lower registers of Newari votive stupas.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone relief of the Buddha Akṣobhya sits in meditative *dhyāna mudrā* within a recessed niche of a 16th-century votive *caitya*. The figure, heavily coated in ritual vermilion powder and yellow pigment, is surrounded by scattered offerings of rice grains and flower petals, reflecting its ongoing role as a focus for daily devotional practice within the sacred precinct.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a frontal eastern perspective, this ornate stone *shikhara*-style Krishna Mandir stands as a masterpiece of Malla-era architecture, characterized by its multi-tiered structure, intricate carvings, and finely detailed stone struts. Dominating the foreground is the monolithic Garuḍa Stambha, topped with a golden figure, while the temple’s upper reaches culminate in a distinctive *āmalaka* and *gajura* pinnacle, all set within the bustling, historic urban fabric of Patan Durbar Square.
A vaishnava pillar in nepal.

This frontal view captures a stone relief of Akṣobhya Buddha carved into a niche, featuring the central deity seated in the *bhūmisparśa mudrā* upon a lotus pedestal, flanked by two standing attendant figures. The weathered stone sculpture, adorned with ritual vermilion powder and floral offerings, is set within a recessed shrine that exhibits the characteristic craftsmanship of Newari Buddhist iconography, reflecting the sacred heritage found near the historic Cīdhã Bahī.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This southeastern perspective of the 1868 CE Bāsimali Phalcā showcases a traditional three-storey Newari structure characterized by exposed red brick masonry and intricate dark-wood lattice windows. The building features a distinctive shed roof supported by six elaborately carved wooden struts, while the foreground is anchored by a stone column topped with a small, caged deity figure, reflecting the site’s dual Buddhist and Hindu religious significance within the historic urban fabric.
A 3-storey buddhist,hindu phalcā in nepal.

This stone relief panel depicts the serpent deity Vāsuki Nāga, seated in a meditative posture and sheltered by a multi-headed cobra canopy. The figure is adorned with intricate jewelry and a subtle red tilaka, emerging from a coiled serpentine base, set within a recessed, lime-washed brick niche that highlights the weathered texture and refined sculptural details of the deity.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This finely carved stone relief depicts a winged, lion-faced deity, likely a form of Siṃha, captured in a reverent *añjali mudrā* posture. Set within a recessed, whitewashed niche, the figure is framed by an inscribed stone backdrop, while the base features stylized lotus petals, reflecting the intricate craftsmanship characteristic of traditional Newari devotional sculpture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a western perspective, this brick-built Vaishnava *phalcā* serves as a modest shrine housing a stone Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa deity within its central niche. The structure features a simple, flat-roofed design topped with a modest *gajura* pinnacle, contrasting with the surrounding traditional Newari residential architecture. Situated on a stone-paved platform, the shrine stands as a quiet remnant of the historical site originally established in 1694 by Dharaṇīdhara.
A vaishnava phalcā in nepal.

This finely carved stone relief panel depicts the divine couple Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa standing in a graceful tribhanga posture, framed by an intricate, flame-bordered toraṇa. The deities are adorned with elaborate jewelry and crowns, while a small devotee figure kneels at the base. The sculpture, weathered and dusted with ritual vermilion powder, remains embedded within a rustic, earth-toned masonry niche.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This eastern corner view of the 1896 Bhailaḥsĩ Bāhāḥ reveals a traditional four-storey Newari monastic courtyard, constructed from exposed brick and intricately carved timber. The facade features ornate jhyāḥ (windows) and a central ground-floor entrance sheltered by a decorative toraṇa, while a stone chaitya sits prominently in the courtyard. Festive prayer flags are strung across the weathered brick walls, highlighting the building's enduring role as a sacred Buddhist space.
A 4-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A stone śikharakūṭa caitya, characterized by its tiered, temple-like structure and intricate relief carvings, stands centrally within a monastic courtyard. The votive monument features a small arched toraṇa housing a deity, surrounded by a metal frame supporting prayer wheels and a perimeter of oil lamp holders, all set against a backdrop of traditional Newari architecture adorned with colorful festive bunting.
A stone śikharakūṭa caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This aniconic stone, revered as a manifestation of Gaṇeśa, is set within a simple, recessed niche. The dark, weathered stone is adorned with a metallic, leaf-shaped crown or mask featuring stylized relief patterns, while traces of vermilion and yellow pigments suggest ongoing ritual devotion. The base is scattered with offerings of white rice grains and fresh marigold petals.
A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Bhaiṣajyarāja Bāhāḥ in Cyāsal reveals a traditional Newari monastic entrance framed by a painted *toraṇa* featuring Buddhist deities. The doorway is flanked by brick masonry and adorned with intricate folk motifs, including parrots, eyes, and ritual vessels. Beyond the threshold, the courtyard reveals the weathered wooden architectural elements of this 1878 CE *bāhāḥ*, highlighting the enduring craftsmanship and sacred spatial organization characteristic of Kathmandu Valley’s historic Buddhist vihāras.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of a deity, likely Mahākāla, is enshrined within a small, arched niche at the Bhaiṣajyarāja Bāhāḥ. The figure is depicted in a robust, frontal posture, heavily adorned with ritual vermilion powder and floral offerings, while the surrounding masonry shows signs of age, moss, and the accumulation of traditional devotional materials at its base.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone caitya, situated within a traditional monastic courtyard, features a central deity sheltered by a multi-headed naga canopy atop a lotus-petal pedestal. The votive structure rests on a tiered base adorned with relief panels and is flanked by guardian lions, all sheltered beneath an ornate, suspended metal canopy that echoes the intricate wood-carved tympanum of the surrounding brick architecture.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Gãdyaḥ Phalcā in Sunakothi reveals a modest, single-storey phalcā—a traditional public rest house—constructed with a simple concrete slab roof, metal-grille facades, and weathered plaster walls adorned with faint graffiti. Situated at a street junction, this utilitarian structure serves as a communal space, contrasting with the surrounding brick masonry buildings, while a stray dog rests quietly on the dusty ground before its unassuming, open-bay entrance.
A 1-storey hindu,buddhist phalcā in nepal.

This corner view of a nineteenth-century Buddhist shrine at Ghana Bāhāḥ reveals a compact, single-storey brick structure topped by a stone *gajura* pinnacle. The central arched niche, framed by an ornate stone *toraṇa* featuring carved deities, houses an image of Akṣobhya Buddha. The monument sits amidst a cluttered courtyard, partially draped in a blue tarp, with its weathered masonry base surrounded by low-lying vegetation and discarded construction materials.
A 1-storey buddhist shrine,monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone image of Akṣobhya Buddha sits in the *bhumisparsha mudra* within a recessed niche of dark, soot-stained brickwork. The deity, adorned with remnants of red *sindur* and floral offerings, rests upon a double-lotus pedestal, flanked by smaller, eroded votive figures and circular stone elements that evoke the sacred, meditative atmosphere of a traditional Newari monastic courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This circular stone base, set within a weathered earthen floor, features an inlaid brass Dharmadhātu Maṇḍala characterized by intricate geometric engravings and sacred iconography. The metal surface, worn and partially obscured by ritual vermilion powder, displays a central square palace structure surrounded by concentric circular motifs, reflecting the profound spiritual significance of this meditative diagram in Newar Buddhist practice.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a weathered, stone Licchavi-period caitya, a classic example of Newari religious architecture. The structure features a square base topped by a tiered plinth and a rounded dome, culminating in a vertical pinnacle or gajura marked with vermilion. A relief carving of a seated Buddha is centered within a niche on the eastern face, while the surrounding courtyard displays a muralled wall, reflecting the site's enduring cultural significance.
A 1-storey hindu-buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This stone votive caitya features a tiered pedestal with relief panels, supporting a lotus-shaped base and a central drum adorned with four seated Buddha figures facing the cardinal directions. Topped by a harmikā and a crowning finial, this weathered monument stands in a residential courtyard, reflecting the enduring tradition of Newari Buddhist devotional architecture amidst the surrounding brick structures.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view of Tāhā Phalcā showcases a traditional two-story Newari structure characterized by exposed red brick masonry and a prominent overhanging hip roof supported by intricately carved wooden struts. The ground floor features an open-air *phalcā* design with dark timber pillars, while the upper level displays finely crafted lattice windows, reflecting the quintessential aesthetic of the Kathmandu Valley’s historic public rest houses.
