A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This gilded, standing stone figure of the disciple Śāriputra is set against a dark, intricately carved wooden relief panel featuring a flame-bordered aureole. The monastic sculpture, displaying a missing right forearm and a traditional alms bowl, rests upon a lotus pedestal, reflecting the refined Newari craftsmanship characteristic of the Cilancva Bāhāḥ temple complex in Kirtipur.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief depicts a monk, likely Maudgalyāyana, standing in a serene posture upon a lotus pedestal against a dark, intricately carved prabhāmaṇḍala (halo) or back-slab. Set against a red brick wall within a Newari monastic courtyard, the figure retains traces of gilding and a red tilaka, though the right arm is damaged, highlighting the devotional character of this architectural sculpture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the historic Yākaḥ Bāhāḥ, a 17th-century Buddhist monastic building in Kirtipur, showcases traditional Newari craftsmanship with its weathered brick masonry and intricate dark-wood carvings. The facade features a central doorway framed by ornate timber moldings, positioned beneath a decorative window assembly that reflects the sophisticated woodwork characteristic of the Malla era, while modest potted marigolds placed along the base provide a subtle, contemporary contrast to the ancient structure.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This weathered stone relief, situated within the courtyard of a Newari Buddhist monastic complex, depicts a stylized elephant serving as a structural support. The sculpture features intricate caparison detailing and traces of ritual vermilion, reflecting its devotional function as a guardian figure positioned at the base of a larger architectural element, likely supporting a shrine or pedestal structure.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone Śikharakūṭa caitya, a classic example of Newari votive architecture, stands within a traditional brick-paved courtyard. The structure features a tiered, multi-roofed design adorned with intricate relief panels and miniature niches, showing signs of weathering and moss growth. It is surrounded by scattered stone fragments, including a small stele, reflecting the layered historical context of this Buddhist monastic setting.
A stone śikharakūṭa caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the 16th-century I Bāhāḥ in Kathmandu showcases a traditional Newari monastic building constructed with exposed red brickwork. The entrance is framed by intricately carved wooden doorjambs and lintels, flanked by a pair of stone guardian lions. Above the doorway, the façade is adorned with a series of small, carved stone heads, reflecting the distinct aesthetic and structural craftsmanship characteristic of historic Buddhist bāhāḥ architecture.
A 4-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone *caitya* stands within a monastic courtyard, featuring a tiered base adorned with relief panels of deities and a central *harmikā* topped by a spire. It is framed by a protective metal structure incorporating stylized *nāga* figures, while a nearby stone lion and a hanging bell reflect the traditional Newari architectural context of the surrounding brick buildings.
A stone vajradhātu caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya, situated within the I Bāhāḥ monastic complex, features a cylindrical drum adorned with weathered relief panels depicting Buddhist deities within arched niches. Topped by a lotus-petal molding, the monument exhibits significant surface erosion, standing in a courtyard setting alongside a larger, more complex stone stupa and a parked motorcycle, reflecting the intersection of historic Newari religious practice and contemporary urban life.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Thapāḥ Bahī, a traditional Buddhist monastic bāhāḥ, showcases a two-storey brick structure featuring a weathered facade with a central wooden window and arched niches above the entrance. In the courtyard, a stone stupa stands as a focal point, topped with a traditional gajura pinnacle, while the surrounding area is cluttered with household items and wooden supports, highlighting the ongoing challenges of preserving this historic cultural site.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of the protective deity Mahākāla is enshrined within a recessed wall niche at Thapāḥ Bahī. The deity is depicted in a standing posture, heavily coated in layers of vermilion and ritual offerings, set against a radiating, flame-like halo that suggests the fierce, transformative energy characteristic of Vajrayāna Buddhist iconography.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone votive caitya stands centrally within a monastic courtyard, featuring a tiered, stepped base adorned with intricate circular relief carvings. The structure is crowned by a faded, polychromed harmikā and finial, while a metal frame with a suspended canopy and small guardian figures encloses the monument, contrasting its ancient, moss-covered masonry with the surrounding modern residential architecture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Majāḥdegaḥ (built sometime between seventeenth and eighteenth century)
captions: This frontal view captures the Majāḥdegaḥ, a historic Buddhist caitya situated on a steep slope, characterized by its tiered stone plinth and a central dome adorned with carvings of Dhyāni Buddhas. The structure is crowned by a traditional gilded pinnacle, or gajura, while the surrounding environment includes a brick-built residential backdrop and overhead utility lines, highlighting the juxtaposition of ancient Newari architectural heritage within a modernizing urban landscape.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This frontal view of the Kusi Bāhāḥ, a 17th-century Buddhist monastic building in Kirtipur, showcases a traditional two-storey brick facade characterized by its austere, exposed masonry. The structure features intricate wooden window frames with delicate lattice screens and a central doorway adorned with a carved wooden lintel, while the ground level remains punctuated by simple doorways, contrasting with the nearby construction materials piled against the weathered exterior wall.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This weathered stone image of the Akṣobhya Buddha sits in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture, atop a tiered lotus pedestal adorned with remnants of polychrome pigment. Housed within a rustic, earthen-walled sanctum, the central deity is flanked by two smaller votive relief panels, reflecting the devotional atmosphere of this traditional Newari monastic setting.
A stone statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone votive caitya stands centrally within a monastic courtyard, featuring a tiered, shikhara-style superstructure adorned with intricate relief panels of seated deities. The structure rests upon a stepped plinth, surrounded by domestic clutter, while the backdrop showcases traditional Newari brickwork, elaborately carved wooden windows, and a decorative frieze, reflecting the layered history of this sacred space.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a northern perspective, this 18th-century Yākaḥ Phalcā stands as a modest, single-bay brick structure featuring traditional Newari craftsmanship, including intricately carved wooden pillars and a decorative frieze. The open-fronted *phalcā* is topped with a simple shed roof, contrasting sharply with the surrounding modern concrete buildings and cluttered urban debris, while the weathered brickwork and exposed structural elements highlight the enduring, yet vulnerable, legacy of traditional Kathmandu Valley architecture.
A 2-storey hindu phalcā in nepal.

monument_name: Bhulukā (Bhulukhā) Degaḥ aka Santāneśvara Mahādeva (supposedly built during the reign of King Prtāpa Malla [1639 – 1674 CE])
captions: This exquisite stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche, depicts the goddess Sarasvatī seated in a graceful posture while playing a vīṇā. The dark, weathered stone surface, accented with ritual vermilion and floral offerings, highlights the intricate carving of her jewelry and the flowing contours of her form, characteristic of the refined craftsmanship found in traditional Newar devotional art.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Bhulukā (Bhulukhā) Degaḥ aka Santāneśvara Mahādeva (supposedly built during the reign of King Prtāpa Malla [1639 – 1674 CE])
captions: A brass ritual bell hangs suspended by iron chains from a weathered stone frame, which features a prominent relief of the sacred Om symbol, a Shaivite tripuṇḍra mark, and a swastika. This devotional structure, situated within a brick-paved courtyard, serves as a functional liturgical element, its austere stone supports contrasting with the intricate woodwork visible in the surrounding temple architecture.
A iron bell at a nepali heritage site.

This southwestern view captures the courtyard of Tana Bāhāḥ, a traditional Buddhist monastic complex featuring a central stone caitya—a miniature stupa form—crowned by a tiered pinnacle. The courtyard is paved with weathered bricks and framed by a white-washed building with residential balconies and a small, distinct shrine. This sacred space, serving as a branch of Oku Bāhāḥ, preserves the intimate, enclosed atmosphere characteristic of historic Newari monastic architecture.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A serene, white-painted stone image of the Buddha Akṣobhya is enshrined within a recessed, glass-fronted wooden niche, characterized by his characteristic earth-touching gesture (*bhūmisparśa mudrā*). The devotional space is adorned with a vibrant, hanging floral garland and fresh offerings, reflecting the ongoing ritual veneration within this traditional Newari Buddhist monastic courtyard setting.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the entrance to Kvātha Bāhāḥ, a traditional Newari monastic courtyard, featuring a finely carved wooden *toraṇa* above the doorway that displays intricate Buddhist iconography. The structure is built with exposed brick masonry and weathered plaster, while the entrance is framed by elaborate, weathered woodwork. Piles of loose bricks rest on the uneven, unpaved ground, highlighting the contrast between the historic architectural craftsmanship and the surrounding state of disrepair.
A buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a traditional Newar monastic courtyard, features a multi-tiered base adorned with relief panels of guardian lions and inscribed mantras. The central structure supports four seated Buddhas sheltered by a canopy, topped by a miniature stupa finial, all exhibiting weathered textures and ritual offerings of white powder, characteristic of ongoing devotional practice.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

This finely carved stone relief features the bodhisattva Padmapāṇi Lokeśvara standing in a graceful tribhanga posture upon a lotus pedestal, set within a recessed niche framed by an ornate, dark-toned toraṇa. Above the central deity, a small Buddha figure sits within a caitya-like arch, while the surrounding relief panels depict intricate mythical creatures and attendants, all enclosed by decorative metal lattice screens.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A central, weathered stone relief of the elephant-headed deity Gaṇeśa sits within a deeply recessed sanctum, heavily coated in layers of vermilion paste and ritual offerings. The figure is framed by an elaborate, gilded brass tympanum featuring intricate floral motifs and a central mask, flanked by two smaller, red-pigmented lion sculptures resting on the floor before a flickering oil lamp.
A brass statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Set within a recessed, rectangular stone niche, these eroded, anthropomorphic stone relief figures are heavily coated in layers of vermilion (sindūra) and adorned with scattered floral offerings. The weathered, fragmented icons, likely representing a deity, are enshrined within a dark, austere masonry chamber, reflecting the long-standing devotional practices common in Kathmandu Valley’s traditional Hindu-Buddhist shrines.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Guitaḥ Gaṇeśa Mandira, a compact, single-storey Newari temple characterized by its intricate wooden lattice work and a traditional hip roof topped with a metallic *gajura* pinnacle. The structure features a central doorway adorned with a carved *toraṇa*, while a row of prayer wheels sits on a metal railing in the foreground, blending the temple’s rich architectural craftsmanship with the surrounding urban residential environment.
A 1-storey hindu-buddhist tiered temple in nepal.

A polychromed stone lion, serving as a protective guardian at the northern side of a temple staircase, sits in a stylized, alert posture. Its body is painted white with prominent black and gold accents, featuring decorative collars and muscular haunches, while the creature rests upon a raised, painted plinth, standing in stark contrast to the intricate, traditional Newari brickwork and carved wooden screens of the surrounding temple architecture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered bronze bell hangs suspended by iron chains within a minimalist concrete frame, topped by a golden finial and a central relief panel depicting the Hindu deity Shiva. Below the bell, a stone śivaliṅga rests on a brick platform, bridging the sacred space between the hanging votive offering and the earth, characteristic of the syncretic devotional sites found in Newari courtyards.
A other pillar,bell at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Licchavi Caitya and statue of Buddha
captions: Captured from a northeastern angle, this weathered stone ensemble rests upon a low, rectangular brick plinth, featuring a classic Licchavi-period caitya alongside a seated Buddha figure. The caitya displays intricate architectural carvings, including miniature niches and a rounded dome, while the adjacent Buddha sits in a meditative posture. Beside them lies a small, detached stone element, all set against a backdrop of construction debris and stacked timber near the Karuṇāmaya Mandira.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

monument_name: Licchavi Caitya and statue of Buddha
captions: This weathered stone relief depicts a seated Buddha figure in a meditative posture, resting upon a pedestal adorned with stylized lotus petal carvings. The sculpture, showing signs of erosion and ritual application of vermilion, is situated outdoors near a brick foundation, reflecting the enduring devotional presence of ancient Buddhist iconography within the Kathmandu Valley’s sacred landscape.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Licchavi Caitya and statue of Buddha
captions: This weathered stone fragment, likely the hemispherical dome or *anda* of a Licchavi-period votive *caitya*, rests upon a stepped stone base alongside a larger relief sculpture. The surface of the dome displays signs of age, including faint remnants of ritual vermilion powder, while a central cavity suggests the former placement of an *harmikā* or pinnacle, reflecting the enduring devotional significance of this ancient Buddhist relic.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the northern wall of Pānda Bāhāḥ in Lukhusi Ṭola showcases a traditional Newari architectural feature, characterized by a recessed doorway framed by intricately carved, teal-painted woodwork. The white-plastered masonry is flanked by two stylized, red-painted zoomorphic figures—reminiscent of mythical creatures often found in Buddhist monastic courtyards—that project horizontally from the structure, emphasizing the building’s distinct religious identity as a site dedicated to the deity Akṣobhya.
A buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief panel of the elephant-headed deity Gaṇeśa, heavily coated in ritual vermilion pigment, is embedded within a recessed niche of a monastic courtyard wall. The deity is depicted in a seated posture, adorned with a single red flower offering, reflecting the enduring devotional practices within the historic Newar Buddhist bāhāḥ architectural tradition.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone *caitya* stands within a monastic courtyard, featuring a multi-tiered structure topped by a finial. The square base displays intricate relief panels depicting guardian lions flanking a central deity, while the upper section showcases a lotus-petal molding and a small niche containing a figure, all exhibiting the weathered texture characteristic of traditional Newari devotional architecture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the eastern facade of Jom Bāhāḥ showcases intricate Newari craftsmanship, featuring a dark, finely carved wooden window frame crowned by a prominent, ornate toraṇa depicting Buddhist deities. The structure is built from traditional red brick, with the window’s lattice screen complemented by sculpted stone figures positioned below, reflecting the building's role as a significant esoteric shrine and monastic residence within the historic urban fabric of Patan.
A 4-storey buddhist esoteric shrine (dyaḥchẽ, āgãchẽ),monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A dark, stone-carved image of the Buddha Akṣobhya sits in meditative repose against a weathered, exposed brick wall within a monastic shrine. The deity is adorned with a metallic, engraved breastplate featuring intricate floral motifs and Devanagari inscriptions, while two small, stylized stone lions flank the base of the pedestal, reflecting the austere, devotional atmosphere of this Newari esoteric space.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone Śikharakūṭa caitya, situated within a traditional Newari courtyard, features a tiered, multi-roofed superstructure adorned with intricate relief panels depicting Buddhist deities within arched niches. The weathered, moss-covered monument stands upon a stepped plinth, surrounded by a metal railing, while the backdrop reveals the characteristic red-brick masonry and carved wooden window screens of the surrounding monastic architecture.
A stone śikharakūṭa caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the ancient stone image of Akṣobhya Buddha housed within a recessed niche of the pre-13th-century Guitaḥ Caitya. The deity, marked by ritual vermilion offerings, sits in the bhūmisparśa mudrā against a carved stone toraṇa. The sanctum is secured by weathered horizontal iron bars, while the surrounding masonry walls, textured by centuries of weathering and scattered ritual pigments, reflect the austere, enduring character of this historic Buddhist site.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This frontal view of the northern facade of Harṣa Bāhāḥ in Patan showcases a quintessential Newari monastic window, characterized by intricate dark-wood carvings set against weathered red brickwork. The central focus is an elaborate *toraṇa* featuring a seated Akṣobhya Buddha flanked by attendants, positioned above a tripartite window arrangement framed by ornate, carved wooden pillars and geometric motifs, reflecting the refined craftsmanship typical of 19th-century Buddhist monastic architecture.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A dark stone statue of the Buddha Akṣobhya sits in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture, atop a lotus pedestal within a monastic shrine. The figure is framed by an elaborate, ornate metal prabhāmaṇḍala (halo) adorned with floral motifs, while a smaller, weathered stone relief panel depicting a devotional figure rests on a stepped platform to the left.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone caitya stands within a Newari courtyard, featuring a multi-tiered base and a central drum adorned with relief carvings of seated deities. The structure is capped by a lotus-petal molding and a small finial, reflecting the intricate craftsmanship typical of nineteenth-century Patan, while the surrounding brick architecture and ornate wooden window frames provide a traditional monastic setting.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view captures the Cyāsal Phalchā, a traditional Newari public rest house integrated into the ground floor of a multi-story brick building. The structure features a prominent open-fronted *phalchā* space defined by light-blue painted pillars, contrasting with the adjacent street-level shops. Beside the stone steps and a tree adorned with ritual red pigment, the weathered facade and scattered masonry reflect the ongoing evolution of this historic urban site.
A integrated phalcā ground floor,phalcā in nepal.

Captured in a frontal view, this weathered Licchavi-period stone caitya stands as a compact, single-tiered Buddhist monument characterized by a broad, square base and a hemispherical dome topped with a damaged pinnacle. The structure features a small, recessed niche on its northern face, while its immediate surroundings are currently cluttered with piles of construction gravel and earth, contrasting the ancient, enduring stone craftsmanship against the backdrop of a traditional brick residential courtyard in Bungmati.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This frontal view captures a stone Pañcāvalīyukta Caitya, a 19th-century Buddhist monument characterized by a tiered, lotus-petaled dome resting upon a square base adorned with intricate relief carvings of Buddhas and Tārās. The structure culminates in a traditional gajura pinnacle, standing amidst a dusty courtyard near a simple wall and construction materials, highlighting the contrast between this ornate, sacred architectural form and the utilitarian, modern surroundings of the site.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

Captured from the southeastern corner, this stone Bhavānīśaṅkara Mandir exhibits the classic North Indian śikhara style, featuring a curvilinear tower topped by a traditional gajura. The structure rests on a multi-tiered plinth, showcasing intricate relief carvings and arched niches. It stands within the historic Bāghabhairava Temple complex, juxtaposed against a large, scaffolded pagoda temple undergoing restoration, while a lone visitor walks past the weathered brick surroundings of the courtyard.
A 1-storey hindu śikhara temple in nepal.

This stone relief panel depicts the divine couple Bhavānīśaṅkara, with Shiva holding a trident and Parvati standing beside him, both adorned with vermilion offerings. Set within a simple arched frame, the deities are flanked by their respective mounts, the bull Nandi and the lion, carved in low relief upon a pedestal within the temple's sanctum.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone relief panel, set within a recessed rectangular masonry frame, depicts a coiled serpent or nāga in low relief. The zoomorphic figure, marked with traces of vermilion ritual offerings, rests on the ground as an aniconic devotional object, reflecting the profound integration of serpentine iconography within the sacred landscape of Newari temple complexes.
A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ī Bahī Vãtā Vihāra (ca. 17th cent. CE)
captions: This side-angle view of the 17th-century Ī Bahī Vãtā Vihāra reveals a series of traditional one-storey monastic structures characterized by weathered red brick masonry and distinctive gable roofs topped with modest pinnacles. These historic buildings, set against a backdrop of modern urban development, feature simple rectangular forms and white-plastered decorative elements, contrasting sharply with the adjacent unfinished concrete frame and the humble, weathered brick boundary wall that encloses the sacred site.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bahī) in nepal.

monument_name: Ī Bahī Vãtā Vihāra (ca. 17th cent. CE)
captions: This stone votive caitya stands within the courtyard of a 17th-century Buddhist bahī, featuring a tiered, stepped base supporting a dome adorned with a central relief panel depicting a seated Buddha. The weathered, dark stone structure exhibits classic Newari craftsmanship, resting upon a broad plinth amidst a traditional brick-paved setting framed by monastic architecture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ī Bahī Vãtā Vihāra (ca. 17th cent. CE)
captions: This stone votive caitya, situated within the courtyard of a 17th-century Newari bahī, features a tiered superstructure resting on a robust plinth adorned with protective leogryphs and central relief carvings. The main body displays a recessed niche housing a deity, framed by a delicate toraṇa, all reflecting the refined craftsmanship and enduring religious traditions of the Kathmandu Valley.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the 18th-century Trivikrama Viṣṇu Mandira in Haugaḥ Ṭola showcases the exquisite craftsmanship of Newari woodcarving, centered on a deeply recessed, ornate doorway topped by a magnificent, multi-layered *toraṇa* depicting the deity. The structure features intricate floral motifs, flanking pilasters, and relief carvings of divine figures, while the interior reveals a stark contrast between the historic, weathered timber facade and the mundane, cluttered storage space within.
A 2-storey vaishnava tiered temple in nepal.

This dark, stone relief panel depicts the multi-armed deity Trivikrama Viṣṇu standing in a dynamic posture, framed by an intricately carved, rounded stele featuring traditional floral and geometric motifs. Coated in ritual red vermilion, the sculpture is currently housed in a cluttered, dimly lit interior space, surrounded by miscellaneous household items and debris that obscure its original devotional context.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a 19th-century Vajradhātu Caitya, a traditional Buddhist monument characterized by its tiered, white-plastered square base and a central hemispherical dome topped with a weathered *gajura* pinnacle. The structure features a recessed niche housing a Dhyānibuddha, flanked by a stone votive slab, set against a backdrop of modern residential buildings and local businesses in a paved courtyard, illustrating the enduring presence of sacred architecture amidst contemporary urban development.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of Amitābha Buddha, depicted in the meditative *dhyāna mudrā*, is enshrined within a recessed niche of a white-washed votive caitya. The figure, marked by ritual applications of vermilion and floral offerings, sits upon a stylized lotus pedestal, framed by a simple arched architectural surround that reflects the ongoing devotional practices of the local community.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A whitewashed stone caitya, characterized by its tiered square base and bell-shaped dome, stands prominently in a paved courtyard alongside a weathered wooden dhvajastambha. This ritual flagpole, secured by stone supports, features a small, ornate wooden shrine box, while the surrounding setting includes traditional brick architecture and a nearby open-sided pavilion, reflecting the enduring devotional landscape of the local community.
A wood ritual object at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the gilded metal icon of Akṣobhya Buddha enshrined within the Ibāḥ Bahī Dune, a 19th-century Buddhist monastic site. The deity wears an ornate crown and is adorned with a necklace of coins, resting against a triangular, lattice-patterned metal mantle. The figure is set against a simple, weathered stone niche, with the surrounding walls marked by remnants of ritual vermilion powder, typical of daily devotional offerings.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This weathered stone relief, situated within a recessed niche, depicts a deity heavily anointed with vermilion paste, obscuring the finer details of its original carving. The iconographic form, likely representing Mahākāla, sits in a meditative or protective posture, surrounded by scattered ritual offerings of rice grains and floral remnants typical of daily Buddhist devotional practices within a Newar monastic courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a Newari monastic courtyard, features a tiered, bell-shaped superstructure resting upon a square base adorned with intricate relief panels of guardian lions. The monument exhibits classic Newari craftsmanship, characterized by delicate floral motifs and a lotus-petal band, reflecting the devotional architectural traditions preserved within the sacred precincts of the Ibāḥ Bahī complex.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal perspective captures the entrance to the 17th-century Sikucā Bāhāḥ in Lalitpur, featuring a traditional Newari torana-adorned gateway flanked by two imposing stone lion guardians. Above the intricate wooden carvings, a pair of deer sculptures flank a central pinnacle, while the courtyard beyond reveals a small golden-roofed mandapa housing a stupa, all set within a dense urban environment characterized by weathered masonry walls and modern residential structures.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This weathered stone relief of the elephant-headed deity Gaṇeśa is enshrined within a recessed, darkened niche, characteristic of Newari devotional spaces. The figure sits in a meditative posture, partially obscured by ritual offerings and layers of vermilion paste, while the surrounding stone surface displays the textured erosion typical of long-term exposure to the elements in a historic monastic courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone caitya, situated within a Newari courtyard, features a tiered square base topped by a miniature stupa structure adorned with weathered relief panels and niches. Enclosed by a simple red metal frame, the votive monument displays moss-covered surfaces and traces of ritual offerings, standing in stark contrast to the modern motorcycles parked nearby in this historic monastic setting.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures two stone icons of Akṣobhya housed within a simple niche at Sijaḥ Bāhāḥ, a 17th-century Buddhist monastic compound. The larger central deity is adorned with a vibrant marigold garland, while both figures are marked with vermilion powder, reflecting ongoing devotional practices. They rest upon a weathered stone plinth scattered with ritual rice offerings, set against the rustic, textured backdrop of a traditional exposed red brick wall.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered brick *caitya* stands within a traditional Newari monastic courtyard, its tiered, square base supporting a central dome crowned by a small, ornate finial. The structure exhibits signs of age with moss-covered surfaces and recessed relief niches, reflecting the architectural vernacular of the surrounding historic brick buildings that characterize this secluded Buddhist monastic compound.
A brick vajradhātu caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This stone *dharmadhātu maṇḍala* sits within a monastic courtyard, featuring a multi-tiered base topped by a double-lotus pedestal. The octagonal central drum displays intricate relief panels depicting deities within arched niches, while the lower section is encircled by a serpent motif, showcasing the refined craftsmanship characteristic of Newari Buddhist devotional art preserved in this historic setting.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This close-up of the southeastern corner of the 19th-century Vajradhara Phalcā reveals the intricate craftsmanship of its traditional Newari construction, featuring a weathered, ornately carved wooden pillar adjacent to a composite wall of aged red bricks and timber beams. The juxtaposition of this historic, sacred architectural element with a discarded computer tower and debris highlights the stark contrast between the decaying cultural heritage of Ghana Bāhāḥ and modern encroachment.
A 1-storey buddhist phalcā in nepal.

Captured from a frontal perspective, this traditional wooden doorway of Duru Bāhāḥ Nani features intricate Newari craftsmanship, characterized by ornate vertical pillars supported by carved stone guardian lions at the base. The structure is framed by a decorative wooden lintel and a central *toraṇa* above the shuttered entrance, which is secured by a simple padlock and adorned with small ritual oil lamps, reflecting the enduring sanctity of this historic 17th-century Buddhist monastic site.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief panel, deeply recessed within a pointed arched niche, depicts a central deity adorned with heavy applications of vermilion powder and fresh floral offerings. The iconographic representation, likely representing a protective guardian or tutelary figure, exhibits stylized drapery and traditional Newari sculptural characteristics, set against a dark, textured stone background that highlights the vibrant ritualistic pigment.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone *caitya* stands at the center of a monastic courtyard, its tiered base and bell-shaped dome adorned with subtle relief carvings and traces of ritual red pigment. Enclosed by a metal frame supporting prayer wheels and rows of small oil lamp holders, this votive monument reflects the enduring devotional practices within the historic Newar Buddhist *bāhāḥ*.
A stone vajradhātu caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured in a frontal view, this stone caitya stands as a compact Buddhist monument characterized by a stepped base and a square harmikā-like structure topped with a weathered, rounded dome and a simple gajura pinnacle. A small, arched niche containing a vermilion-anointed deity figure serves as the central focal point, while the surrounding environment includes a pile of discarded bricks and a corrugated metal fence, highlighting the contrast between ancient heritage and modern encroachment.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

Captured from the southwestern side, this mid-17th-century Gaṇeśa shrine in Bungamati features a modest, single-story form topped by a weathered hip roof of traditional terracotta tiles. The structure displays a distinct contrast between its historic brick-and-timber core—characterized by intricate lattice screens—and the makeshift corrugated metal and bamboo extensions surrounding it, which now serve as domestic storage, highlighting the evolving, layered nature of living heritage within the Newari settlement.
A 1-storey hindu shrine in nepal.

This weathered stone relief of Gaṇeśa is enshrined within a recessed, arched niche flanked by two miniature, tiered brick caityas. The deity, heavily coated in vermilion paste and adorned with a marigold garland, sits amidst soot-stained walls, while small iron bells hang from the flanking structures, reflecting the enduring devotional practices within this traditional Newari courtyard shrine.
A iron shrine at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone caitya stands as a central votive monument within a rustic residential courtyard in Bungamati. The structure features a tiered base with inset relief panels depicting deities, including a prominent Gaṇeśa, while the upper dome is crowned by a traditional finial, reflecting the enduring integration of Buddhist architectural forms within this historic Newar settlement.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from the northwestern corner, this 1590 CE Narasiṃha Mandira showcases a classic brick-built *śikhara* style, characterized by its soaring, curvilinear tower topped with a stone *āmalaka* and *gajura*. The structure features intricate white-washed niches, carved wooden columns, and a base surrounded by scattered stone tiles and construction scaffolding, reflecting ongoing restoration efforts for this significant Vaishnava monument that stands as a testament to Lalitpur’s rich architectural heritage.
A 1-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

This corner view of the Nāsaḥ Phalcā in Cyāsal showcases a traditional single-storey *phalcā* characterized by its distinctive lean-to roof covered in terracotta tiles. The structure features dark, intricately carved wooden pillars and decorative struts supporting the overhanging eaves, set against a rustic red brick wall. A weathered stone plaque and a faded festival poster adorn the exterior, reflecting the building's enduring role as a communal space within the historic Newari urban fabric.
A 1-storey hindu,buddhist integrated phalcā ground floor,phalcā in nepal.

This western-facing perspective captures the interior of the Āratī Chẽ, a traditional Newari capāḥ or ceremonial rest house. The structure features a robust wooden framework supported by intricately carved dark timber columns, contrasting sharply against the exposed red brick masonry walls. The open-plan ground floor, defined by its rhythmic beam-and-post construction and wooden flooring, serves as a transitional space that bridges the quiet interior with the bustling exterior surroundings.
A 2-storey capāḥ in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of a Dhyani Buddha, seated in the meditative *dhyana mudra* upon a lotus pedestal, is enshrined within a simple, pointed-arch niche set into a rustic brick wall. Adorned with traces of vermilion powder and ritual offerings, this devotional image reflects the quiet, enduring spiritual presence characteristic of the historic Newari architectural landscape.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Two stone donor figures, carved in a traditional kneeling posture with hands pressed together in anjali mudra, flank the entrance of a Newari Buddhist monastery. These weathered sculptures, adorned with traces of vermilion and marigold offerings, rest upon a plinth inscribed with Newari script, set against a backdrop of intricate, dark-stained wooden architectural carvings.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a tiered base adorned with intricate relief carvings and a central pedestal supporting a seated deity. The structure is crowned by a ceremonial red canopy, while the weathered stone surface, marked by ritual pigments, stands in stark contrast to the surrounding stacks of traditional red bricks.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a stone *chaturmukha caitya*, a classic Newari Buddhist monument featuring four seated Buddha figures carved into its central *harmikā* section. The structure rests on a tiered, square plinth adorned with intricate relief carvings, topped by a lotus-petal base and a crowning *gajura* pinnacle. Situated amidst a dusty, urban setting with scattered bricks and a parked motorcycle, this weathered votive shrine exemplifies traditional Kathmandu Valley craftsmanship.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

monument_name: Kani Bāhāḥ at Oku Bāhāḥ (1702 CE)
captions: This frontal view of the northern facade of Kani Bāhāḥ, a 1702 CE Buddhist monastic building, showcases exquisite Newari craftsmanship characterized by intricate dark-wood carvings. The central entrance features a lattice-work door framed by ornate moldings, flanked by two symmetrical windows with decorative latticed screens. Above, a heavy wooden lintel and a series of carved struts support the overhanging roof, while a small prayer wheel is mounted to the right wall.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

monument_name: Kani Bāhāḥ at Oku Bāhāḥ (1702 CE)
captions: Set within a recessed, vermilion-stained stone niche, this dynamic relief sculpture depicts the wrathful deity Mahākāla in a vigorous dancing posture, his form heavily coated in ritual offerings. A small, weathered bronze bell hangs from a chain at the left, while scattered flower petals and rice grains at the base underscore the ongoing devotional activity within this monastic architectural setting.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Kani Bāhāḥ at Oku Bāhāḥ (1702 CE)
captions: This intricately carved stone caitya, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a tiered base adorned with relief panels depicting Buddhist motifs. The central structure displays four arched niches containing red-painted deities, while the upper dome is crowned by a traditional finial, reflecting the sophisticated Newari craftsmanship typical of eighteenth-century votive architecture in the Kathmandu Valley.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a weathered stone relief of the deity Akṣobhya, enshrined within a recessed niche of the historic Haugaḥ Bāhāḥ in Patan. The deity is depicted in a dynamic posture, framed by the intricate, textured masonry characteristic of this 17th-century Buddhist monastic complex. The sculpture, protected behind glass, displays traditional Newari craftsmanship, showcasing the refined stone-carving traditions that define the architectural heritage of the Kathmandu Valley.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This dark stone relief panel, set within a red brick wall, depicts a standing Lokeśvara flanked by the deity Vasundharā. The figures are framed by a finely carved *toraṇa* featuring a central lotus motif and elaborate scrolling foliage. Protected behind glass, the weathered sculpture displays traditional Newari iconographic features and a faint dedicatory inscription along its base.
A statue at a nepali heritage site.

A stone caitya stands centrally within a traditional Newari courtyard, featuring a stepped plinth, a vibrant lotus-petal base, and a white dome topped by a gilded finial. The structure includes a small, arched niche housing a relief image of a seated Buddha, set against a weathered brick backdrop adorned with intricate wooden lattice windows and historic doorways.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Bhājudhana Bāhāḥ in Patan showcases an exquisite wooden *toraṇa* crowning the entrance, intricately carved with a central Garuda figure flanked by serpentine deities and a triad of seated Buddhas. Below this arch, a small, vermilion-anointed votive figure sits amidst the dark, weathered timber facade, which is further embellished by traditional Newari geometric patterns and ornate vertical struts characteristic of this historic Buddhist monastic courtyard.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone relief panel depicts the wrathful deity Mahākāla standing in a dynamic posture atop a prostrate figure, his form heavily adorned with vermilion powder and floral offerings. The deity holds a ritual staff in his left hand, set within a recessed, dark stone niche that serves as a devotional shrine within the monastic courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A finely carved stone votive caitya stands within a traditional Newari monastic courtyard, featuring a tiered pedestal adorned with intricate relief panels and guardian figures. The central structure supports a lotus base topped by four seated Buddha figures facing the cardinal directions, culminating in a slender finial, all set against a backdrop of historic brick architecture and ornate wooden windows.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This western-angle view captures a prayer wheel shrine within the Khāchẽ Bāhāḥ, featuring a square, multi-colored metal frame protected by a tiered, pagoda-style golden roof topped with a small gajura. Intricate, painted metal latticework panels decorate the sides, while traditional prayer wheels rotate within the structure, set against the rustic, exposed brick walls and ornate, dark-carved wooden door frames characteristic of this historic Newari Buddhist monastic courtyard.
A buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A weathered, cast-brass elephant figure stands atop a decorative, lotus-petaled pedestal, serving as a symbolic guardian within the sacred interior of a Newari Buddhist bāhāḥ. The small, dust-covered statue displays a stylized, upright posture with its trunk slightly curled, reflecting the intricate metalwork traditions often found adorning the base of a dhvajastambha or surrounding a central caitya in monastic courtyards.
A brass statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered brass sculpture, situated within a Newari Buddhist monastic courtyard, depicts a stylized guardian lion standing in a dynamic, upright posture. Its intricate, cast-metal form features traditional zoomorphic iconography and ornate decorative flourishes, showing significant signs of age, dust, and oxidation, reflecting its enduring role as a devotional fixture within the sacred space of the bāhāḥ.
A brass statue at a nepali heritage site.

This southeastern-angled view showcases the Kapileśvara Mahādeva Mandira, a 17th-century Shaiva temple characterized by its stone *śikhara* superstructure. The structure features an ornate arched doorway framed by a decorative *toraṇa*, while the tapering tower culminates in a traditional *gajura* pinnacle. Situated amidst the dense urban fabric of Kathmandu, the temple stands in stark contrast to the surrounding modern brick buildings and traditional wooden *pati* structures nearby.
A 1-storey shaiva śikhara temple in nepal.

A weathered stone sculpture of the sacred bull Nandī, the vahana of Shiva, rests in a recumbent posture within the temple courtyard. The figure features a prominent hump, a beaded collar, and traces of ritual vermilion and yellow marigold offerings, exhibiting the modest, devotional aesthetic characteristic of traditional Newari stone carving found in historic Shaiva temple precincts.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel depicts a seated Gaṇeśa, characterized by his characteristic elephant head, trunk, and robust form, set within a recessed niche featuring a decorative, scalloped border. The deity is adorned with traditional ornaments and is presented in a devotional context, showing signs of ritual offerings, including floral tributes, within the temple’s courtyard setting.
A brick shrine,statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a western perspective, this stone pillar features a central relief carving of the deity Annapūrṇa, framed by an intricately sculpted *toraṇa* archway. The figure stands upon a multi-layered pedestal, adorned with ritual vermilion offerings, while the backdrop reveals the traditional red-brick masonry and wooden louvered windows of the surrounding Patan Darbar Square, emphasizing the integration of this commemorative monument within the historic urban fabric of the city.
A pillar in nepal.

This frontal view captures the Dharmadhātujinālaya Caitya, a stone Buddhist monument situated in Kotā Ṭola. The structure features a square plinth adorned with relief carvings, supporting a prominent lotus-petal base and a central cylindrical body featuring seated Buddha figures. Above, the tiered structure culminates in a traditional finial, likely representing a *gajura*. The caitya stands against a backdrop of traditional brick masonry architecture, characteristic of the historic Newari urban landscape.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This frontal view of the Baku Nani courtyard entrance in Itum Bāhāḥ Ṭola reveals a traditional Newari architectural layout, flanked by a closed metal shutter shop and a small open-fronted stall. Above the central passage, a small, recessed niche houses a sacred deity, while the weathered brick facade and complex overhead wiring contrast with the serene, stone-paved interior path leading toward the historic Aśoka Caitya, a significant Buddhist monument within this ancient urban space.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This stone caitya, situated within the courtyard of Itum Bāhāḥ, features a tiered, bell-shaped dome adorned with intricate lotus-petal carvings and a central, weathered śivaliṅga. Resting upon a robust, molded pedestal, the votive stupa exhibits signs of age and ritual use, with remnants of vermilion and yellow pigment highlighting its delicate relief work against the plain, interior architectural setting.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ṭīkā Bhairava at Bhelachẽ (c. mid-16th cent. CE)
captions: Captured from a western perspective, the Ṭīkā Bhairava temple in Bhelāchẽ stands as a classic three-tiered pagoda structure featuring a tiered roof system topped with a metallic gajura pinnacle. Its brick masonry base is enclosed by a dark metal grille, while the upper levels showcase intricate wooden struts supporting the eaves. The temple is nestled within a dense urban setting, flanked by traditional Newari architecture and a nearby pati, reflecting the historic character of Patan.
A 3-storey hindu tiered temple in nepal.

monument_name: Ṭīkā Bhairava at Bhelachẽ (c. mid-16th cent. CE)
captions: A weathered stone sculpture of Nandi, the sacred bull and vehicle of Shiva, rests in a recumbent posture between two dark stone pillars. The figure’s head is heavily coated in vibrant red vermilion powder, a common devotional practice, while scattered flower petals and grains of rice on the stone floor indicate ongoing ritual veneration within this historic temple precinct.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ṭīkā Bhairava at Bhelachẽ (c. mid-16th cent. CE)
captions: A heavy, dark stone frame supports a suspended bronze bell, crowned by a carved stone finial resembling a stylized *caitya* or lotus motif. Beneath the bell, a weathered, vermilion-smeared stone *śivaliṅga* rests on the paved ground, creating a devotional focal point within this traditional Newari urban courtyard, where the juxtaposition of ancient ritual objects contrasts with the surrounding residential architecture.
A stone bell at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a finely carved wooden niche set into the white-washed wall of Dune Bāhāḥ, a historic Buddhist monastic building. The structure features a prominent, intricately sculpted *toraṇa* crowning the recess, which houses a weathered stone relief of Lord Ganesha adorned with vermilion. Flanked by decorative pillars, this sacred alcove reflects the exquisite Newari craftsmanship characteristic of the 17th-century monastic architecture found within the vibrant Okubāhāl Ṭola.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This brick-and-mortar votive caitya, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a central shrine with a barred doorway topped by an intricate stone toraṇa. The structure is adorned with relief panels of deities and guardian lions, while a surrounding metal railing holds numerous small oil lamps, highlighting the ongoing devotional practices amidst the weathered architectural setting.
A stone shrine,statue at a nepali heritage site.

A stone caitya stands centrally within a monastic courtyard, featuring a tiered, multi-faceted superstructure adorned with relief panels of seated deities. The monument is enclosed by a metal frame supporting numerous small oil lamp holders, while a weathered, circular stone base sits in the foreground, set against the backdrop of traditional Newari brick architecture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a high-angle, downward perspective, this image reveals the weathered stone foundation and recessed entrance of the Taleju Bell structure. The masonry consists of rough-hewn, light-colored stone blocks forming a deep, shadowed pit leading to the interior. The surrounding surface displays the characteristic textures of ancient Newari stonework, emphasizing the rugged, subterranean nature of this historic site, which serves as a foundational element for the monument's prominent metal-roofed superstructure.
A 1-storey buddhist,hindu heritage monument in nepal.

Captured from a southern perspective, this stone *chaitya*—a miniature *stupa*—features a tiered, square base and a bell-shaped dome topped with a weathered *gajura* pinnacle. A small, recessed niche at its center enshrines an image of the Buddha, likely Akṣobhya. This sacred monument sits in a state of disrepair amidst construction debris and wooden scaffolding, set against the backdrop of a traditional brick-built *bāhāḥ* structure in the historic urban landscape.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a cluttered courtyard, features a tiered superstructure topped by a finial and a central niche housing a standing deity. The structure is adorned with intricate relief carvings, including a miniature Buddha in a tympanum above a larger, red-pigmented figure framed by a decorative toraṇa, all showing signs of weathering amidst surrounding construction debris.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This stone *caitya*, situated within a courtyard, features a tiered base adorned with relief panels depicting deities framed by arched niches. The structure is crowned by a prominent lotus-petal capital, while the central register displays intricate inscriptions. Currently weathered and surrounded by debris, this votive monument reflects the traditional Newari craftsmanship characteristic of Buddhist monastic courtyards in the Kathmandu Valley.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Yatkha Bāhāḥ Caitya displays a prominent, whitewashed Buddhist stupa standing centrally within a traditional Newari courtyard. The structure features a hemispherical dome topped by a golden harmikā with painted eyes, crowned by a multi-tiered pinnacle or gajura. Surrounding the base are small shrines housing Dhyānī Buddhas, while the weathered brick buildings of the courtyard provide a dense, historic backdrop to this sacred religious monument.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

A vibrant, painted terracotta relief of a kneeling deity in an añjali mudrā (gesture of reverence) is set against the white, curved base of a votive caitya. The figure, adorned with a floral garland, features stylized facial features and traditional ornaments, emerging from a dark, textured backdrop that contrasts with the smooth, whitewashed masonry of the surrounding Buddhist monument.
A terracotta statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel depicts a Buddha seated in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture, set within a recessed niche of a caitya. The figure, adorned with a sanghāti robe and stylized hair, is heavily coated in vermilion powder and scattered floral offerings, reflecting its ongoing role as a focus for daily devotional worship in the courtyard.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This northern-side view captures the intricate craftsmanship of the 1704 CE Dabū Phalcā, a traditional Newari Buddhist public rest house. The structure features a heavy, dark-wood timber frame with elaborate carvings, including ornate struts supporting the projecting eaves. Below the gablet roof, a metal-grated opening reveals the interior, while the ground level is cluttered with storage sacks, contrasting the historic architectural elegance with the vibrant, lived-in reality of the Thati courtyard.
A 2-storey buddhist phalcā in nepal.

This dark stone relief panel, set within a recessed brick niche, depicts a standing Lokeśvara figure adorned with an ornate crown and jewelry. The deity is framed by a delicate, flame-bordered toraṇa and stands upon a stylized lotus pedestal, flanked by smaller attendant figures, showcasing the refined sculptural traditions of the Newar artisans within this historic Buddhist phalcā.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Nārāyaṇa Mandira, a single-storey Vaishnava temple within the Bāghabhairava complex in Kirtipur, characterized by its white-washed masonry walls and a distinct shikhara-style roof crowned with a golden gajura. The structure features an intricately carved black wooden doorway flanked by red panels, while a smaller, weathered stone shrine stands nearby, all set within a traditional courtyard surrounded by brick residential buildings and a young child playing.
A 1-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

A stone relief of Śrīdharaviṣṇu stands in the sanctum, depicted in a rigid, frontal posture against a flame-bordered stele. The deity is heavily coated in vermilion powder, obscuring fine details of the carved attributes and lotus pedestal, while scattered floral offerings lie at the base of this devotional icon within the rustic brick-walled shrine.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel depicts the goddess Sarasvatī standing in a graceful tribhanga posture, holding her signature vīṇā across her torso. Housed within a simple niche, the deity is heavily coated in ritual vermilion (sindūra) and offerings, with her form carved in a traditional style that emphasizes the iconographic attributes of the Hindu goddess of wisdom and music.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This western-side view captures the Vimaleśvara Mahādeva Mandira, a compact, single-story Shaiva temple characterized by its distinctive stone dome and a prominent āmalaka pinnacle. The structure features a colonnaded exterior with simple pillars, while the surrounding brick-paved plinth serves as a vibrant space for local commerce, where a vendor sits amidst marigold garlands, contrasting with the traditional Newari pagoda-style architecture of the adjacent multi-tiered building looming in the background.
A 1-storey shaiva domed temple in nepal.

A stone sculpture of the sacred bull Nandī, the mount of Lord Śiva, rests in a kneeling posture before a Shaiva temple. The dark, weathered stone figure is adorned with vibrant marigold garlands draped around its neck and back, while scattered flower petals and plastic bags nearby indicate ongoing devotional activity in this busy, contemporary urban setting.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche, depicts a four-armed female deity adorned with an ornate crown and jewelry. Framed by a decorative arch featuring repeating circular motifs, the figure displays a gesture of reassurance with her primary right hand, while her remaining hands hold ritual attributes, reflecting the refined craftsmanship characteristic of traditional Newari architectural ornamentation.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

The Cibhāḥkhyaḥ Caitya, captured from its southeastern corner, features a prominent white-domed stūpa resting upon a tiered, red-painted plinth, topped with a gilded, multi-ringed pinnacle known as a *gajura*. This Buddhist monument is enclosed by a vibrant blue metal fence, while a nearby *pati*-style shelter houses a golden Buddha statue, all set against the dense, multi-story brick residential architecture characteristic of the historic Kaṅkeśvarī neighborhood in Kathmandu.
A 1-storey buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This weathered stone relief depicts the Buddha Akṣobhya seated in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture, within a recessed niche. The deity is framed by a simple, rounded arch, with remnants of ritual vermilion and yellow pigments adorning the face and lap, reflecting its ongoing veneration as a central devotional icon within the historic Cibhāḥkhyaḥ Caitya pavilion.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Two stone votive caityas stand prominently in a paved courtyard, each featuring a tiered, square base adorned with intricate relief panels and inscribed Sanskrit mantras. The central caitya displays a crowned, multi-faced deity sheltered by a naga canopy, topped by a miniature sikhara, all set against a vibrant red brick wall and a contrasting blue metal fence.
A stone dharmadhātujinālaya caitya at a nepali heritage site.

A side-profile view captures a stone statue of Śākyamuni Buddha seated in meditation, housed within a protective yellow metal cage topped by a traditional gilded stupa pinnacle, or gajura. This modest shrine stands amidst the urban landscape of Balambu, where the dark, weathered stone of the deity contrasts with the surrounding modern residential buildings, utility wires, and the nearby tiled roof of a traditional temple structure.
A buddhist shrine in nepal.

This dark, finely carved stone image of the Śākyamuṇi Buddha sits in the *bhūmisparśa mudrā* (earth-touching gesture), resting upon a stylized lotus pedestal. Enclosed within a protective metal cage, the serene figure features traditional cranial protuberances and flowing monastic robes, standing as a prominent devotional icon amidst the rustic, exposed brickwork of its contemporary shrine setting in Balambu.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southwestern elevated perspective, this historic *hiti* (sunken water conduit) in Bhīmasenasthāna features deep, tiered brick retaining walls that descend into a stone-paved courtyard. The site incorporates traditional *pati* (public rest pavilions) and stone *makara* spouts, now juxtaposed with modern plastic water storage tanks and containers. This architectural space, serving as a vital communal water source, reflects the enduring intersection of ancient Newari hydraulic engineering and contemporary urban necessity.
A buddhist,hindu water architecture in nepal.

This weathered stone relief panel, enshrined within a simple architectural niche beneath a small, rounded caitya-like structure, depicts the divine couple Umāmaheśvara in a traditional lalitasana pose. The deities, adorned with traces of vermilion, are framed by a weathered, arched toraṇa, showcasing the enduring devotional significance of this sculptural fragment integrated into the historic water architecture of Kathmandu.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone śivaliṅga, featuring a cylindrical shaft resting upon a tiered, circular yoni-pīṭha, stands against a moss-covered brick wall adorned with a horizontal relief panel of repeating floral motifs. The sacred object, showing signs of erosion and organic growth, sits within the historic Bhindyaḥ Hiti, reflecting the enduring syncretic devotional traditions of the Kathmandu Valley.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a northwestern angle, the Siddhimukteśvara Mandira stands as a distinctive 19th-century Shaiva shrine characterized by its elegant, white-plastered dome topped with a traditional gajura pinnacle. The structure features a square base with vibrant red-painted masonry, contrasting sharply with the utilitarian water tanks and chain-link fencing in the foreground. This domed temple, situated near the Pacalī Ghāṭa, exemplifies a blend of classical architectural influences within the sacred riverbank landscape.
A 1-storey hindu,shaiva domed temple in nepal.

This stone śivaliṅga, housed within the sanctum of a nineteenth-century domed temple, rests upon a circular yoni-pīṭha base. The aniconic form is adorned with a floral offering and surrounded by small metallic coins and a green bean, while a small copper vessel sits nearby, reflecting the humble, ongoing devotional practices characteristic of this sacred space.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southeastern angle, the Gopāleśvara Mandira stands as a single-story Shaiva temple nestled within a dense, verdant forest. The structure features a prominent hemispherical dome topped with a traditional *gajura* pinnacle, resting atop white-washed brick walls adorned with arched doorways and delicate *toraṇa* elements. This secluded shrine, marking the cremation site of the sage Gopāla Muni, harmoniously blends its weathered masonry with the surrounding natural landscape.
A 1-storey shaiva domed temple in nepal.

A weathered stone deity, likely representing a form of Shiva, sits in a recessed earthen niche, adorned with a sacred thread and traces of ritual pigments. In the foreground, a brass oil lamp (dīpa) glows before the image, while a smaller, multi-armed polychrome sculpture stands to the side, reflecting the intimate, devotional atmosphere of this sacred site.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This anthropomorphic *mukhaliṅga* features a serene, carved face emerging from a dark stone cylindrical shaft, adorned with a garland of *rudrākṣa* beads and vibrant red floral offerings. Set within a rustic, earthen-plastered shrine, the icon rests upon a circular *yoni* base, accompanied by a small metallic vessel and a lit oil lamp, evoking a profound sense of ascetic devotion.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered, upright stone pillar stands beside a rugged mountain path, exhibiting a tall, rectangular form with a subtly tapered, rounded top. Its surface, heavily textured by lichen and age, displays faint, eroded relief carvings that hint at traditional votive iconography, while its placement alongside a dry-stone wall suggests its historical function as a commemorative marker within a sacred landscape.
A stone pillar at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered, upright stone monolith stands along a rugged mountain path, its surface heavily colonized by lichen and showing signs of significant erosion. The slab features a rounded, slightly pointed top and a flattened, elongated form, characteristic of the votive memorial pillars found near Thokreya, which serve as silent, weathered sentinels marking the landscape in this remote Himalayan setting.
A stone pillar at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Hitigāḥcā (built during the late Malla Period)
captions: This high-angle view captures a traditional Newari *hiti* (stone water spout), a late Malla-period architectural marvel constructed from weathered grey stone blocks. The sunken rectangular basin features tiered steps leading down to a central stone spout, adorned with a subtle, worn *makara* or animal-headed water outlet. Surrounded by paved stone platforms and a brick retaining wall, this cultural landmark reflects the sophisticated hydraulic engineering and communal heritage of the Kathmandu Valley.
A hindu,buddhist water architecture in nepal.

monument_name: Hitigāḥcā (built during the late Malla Period)
captions: This weathered stone relief, carved in low relief, depicts the ascetic figure of Bhagīratha standing in a meditative posture within a recessed niche. This stone relief panel features a pair of *Viṣṇupādukā*, or sacred footprints, carved in low relief upon a circular pedestal framed by a decorative beaded border. This frontal view of the Guitaḥ Capāḥ in Lalitpur showcases a four-storey structure featuring a modern concrete slab roof and a prominent mural depicting scenes from the Ramayana, including the shikhara-style architecture of Ayodhya and forest hermitages. The southern side of the Hanumān shrine features a prominent, orange-vermilion idol of the deity sheltered beneath a simple, metal-roofed mandapa structure.
