This brass statue of the wrathful deity Bhairava, set within a tiered temple, features a dynamic, multi-armed form adorned with intricate relief work and a prominent, ornate *toraṇa* arch. The figure is heavily layered with vermilion powder and floral offerings, resting upon a lotus-petal base amidst scattered ritual remnants, reflecting the vibrant, ongoing devotional practices characteristic of the Kathmandu Valley’s sacred sites.
A brass statue at a nepali heritage site.

A small, red-painted brick shrine stands in an open field, featuring a simple arched entrance that frames a gilded relief panel of Gaṇeśa. In front of the structure, a weathered stone vahana (mount) of a mouse rests atop a pedestal, while a nearby dhvajastambha suggests the sacred nature of this site within the broader Kathmandu Valley landscape.
A brass statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

This northeastern corner view captures the 17th-century Cāṅgala Bāhāḥ, a compact, single-storey stone Buddhist shrine characterized by its distinctive *shikhara*-style roof and intricate masonry. The structure features a recessed, barred entrance and refined decorative moldings, currently nestled amidst a chaotic urban construction site filled with gravel piles and debris. Despite the encroaching modern development and tangled overhead power lines, the shrine retains its historic architectural integrity and sacred presence.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A stone relief of the Buddha Akṣobhya sits in the *bhumisparsha mudra* within a recessed shrine, framed by a weathered, yellow-pigmented *toraṇa* featuring a central *kīrtimukha* mask. The figure, marked with ritual vermilion, rests upon a lotus pedestal, surrounded by faint, eroded attendant carvings, reflecting the devotional continuity of this small, historic Newar monastic site.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief panel depicts a standing figure of Padmapāṇi Lokeśvara, characterized by his graceful tribhanga posture and lotus stalks held in both hands. Set within a recessed niche, the deity’s face is obscured by ritual vermilion paste and a floral offering, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices that have softened the sculpture’s original fine details over centuries.
A statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southeastern angle, this 19th-century Kṛṣṇa Mandira stands as a modest, single-storey domed structure atop a prominent red-painted plinth. The temple features a central entrance adorned with a traditional *toraṇa* above its metal-grille door and a distinct *gajura* pinnacle crowning the roof. A ritual bell hangs from a simple wooden frame, while the surrounding urban landscape of brick buildings contrasts with the temple’s austere, elevated form and surrounding greenery.
A 2-storey hindu,vaishnava domed temple in nepal.

Within the sanctum of a ruined 19th-century Vaishnava temple, three bronze figures stand upon a shared, lotus-petaled pedestal against a weathered, exposed brick wall. The central deity, depicted as Kṛṣṇa playing a flute, is flanked by his consorts, all adorned in vibrant orange and gold garments, representing a poignant remnant of devotional artistry preserved amidst the monument's architectural collapse.
A bronze statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone sculpture of Garuda, the divine avian mount of Vishnu, kneels in anjali mudra atop a lotus-pedestal base. Characterized by expansive wings, ornate jewelry, and a serene countenance, this anthropomorphic figure exhibits the refined craftsmanship of Newari tradition, standing as a solitary devotional remnant amidst the urban surroundings of a once-grand nineteenth-century Vaishnava temple complex.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from the southern side, the 16th-century Śrīghaḥ Caitya stands as a prominent Buddhist monument featuring a massive, whitewashed hemispherical dome topped by a square harmikā adorned with the all-seeing eyes of Amitābha. The structure is crowned by a tiered, golden gajura pinnacle and encircled by prayer wheels, while vibrant prayer flags radiate outward, contrasting against the surrounding urban brick buildings and traditional Newari architecture that define this historic Kathmandu courtyard.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

Set within a recessed white-plastered niche, this weathered stone relief panel depicts a pair of kneeling donor figures in the traditional *añjali mudrā* gesture of reverence. Their stylized forms, characterized by ornate headgear and draped garments, rest upon a lotus-petal base, reflecting the devotional iconography commonly found adorning the periphery of a sacred Buddhist *caitya* complex.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Set against the weathered white dome of a votive caitya, this stone shrine dedicated to Amitābha features a multi-tiered, pagoda-style roof structure. The central niche, secured by iron lattice and a padlock, is crowned by an ornate, semi-circular toraṇa displaying intricate relief carvings, while flanking panels depict standing deities, showcasing the refined craftsmanship of traditional Newari architectural ornamentation.
A stone shrine,statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of Tamvaḥ Bāhāḥ in Kathmandu showcases a traditional four-storey Newari monastic building constructed from exposed red brick and intricate dark-stained timber. The structure features a prominent, ornate wooden balcony with delicate lattice work, while a central, metal-roofed mandapa shelters a stone stupa and a small, gilded gajura pinnacle. The courtyard’s checkered stone floor and surrounding urban architecture frame this historic Buddhist site, highlighting its enduring cultural significance.
A 4-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This dark, finely carved stone relief depicts a standing male figure, likely the disciple Sāriputra, holding a staff and bowl atop a lotus pedestal. Set against a weathered brick wall, the sculpture is framed by an ornate, flame-bordered stela and positioned beside a gilded, multi-tiered dhvajastambha, reflecting the refined devotional aesthetics of Newari Buddhist monastic architecture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Standing prominently in a paved courtyard, this intricately carved stone *caitya* features a multi-tiered base and a central cubical structure adorned with relief panels of deities. The votive stupa is capped by a traditional tiered finial, while the surrounding black metal gate and brick architecture provide a stark, historical backdrop to this fine example of Newari devotional sculpture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered, white-painted stone lion, serving as a protective guardian at the entrance of a Newari Buddhist *bāhāḥ*, stands in a stylized, upright posture on a moss-covered plinth. Its simplified, robust form reflects traditional iconographic conventions, showing signs of age and repeated layers of pigment while positioned against a backdrop of historic brickwork and a metal lattice screen.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a monastic courtyard, features a smooth, plastered surface with two recessed niches housing small, weathered relief figures. The structure is sheltered by a simple wooden canopy and rests upon a stone plinth, surrounded by remnants of ritual offerings, reflecting its ongoing role in the daily devotional life of the Newar Buddhist community.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the northern side of Chāya Bāhāḥ showcases a traditional Newari monastic entrance, characterized by its weathered red brick masonry and intricate black-painted wooden door frame. Above the latticed door, a finely carved wooden toraṇa depicts Buddhist iconography, while the façade is punctuated by small arched niches and a repeating Sanskrit inscription, reflecting the spiritual heritage of this 17th-century Buddhist bāhāḥ nestled within the historic urban fabric.
A 3-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

A stone image of a deity, likely a form of the Buddha or Bodhisattva, sits enthroned upon a tiered pedestal within a monastic interior, draped in a vibrant, gold-patterned red textile. Below the primary figure, a collection of small, weathered stone votive caityas and ritual objects rests on the base, reflecting the devotional practices maintained within this traditional Newari Buddhist bāhāḥ.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Set within a tiled, arched niche in a Newari courtyard, a weathered stone relief of a multi-armed deity, likely Vasundharā, sits centrally upon a lotus pedestal. The shrine features a small hanging bell, a rudimentary light fixture, and flanking wooden boxes, contrasting the ancient, pigment-stained iconography with the utilitarian, contemporary additions typical of active, lived-in monastic spaces.
A stone shrine,statue at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view of the 1703 CE Umāmaheśvara Degaḥ captures a classic stone śikhara-style temple, characterized by its tapering tower topped with a traditional gajura pinnacle. The structure features an open-pillared base supporting the main sanctum, which is adorned with intricate stone carvings, including a central relief of the deity. The temple stands in stark contrast to the surrounding modern residential buildings, amidst a foreground cluttered with construction debris and salvaged materials.
A shaiva śikhara temple in nepal.

This stone relief panel depicts the divine couple Umāmaheśvara seated in lalitāsana beneath a finely carved, pointed arch or toraṇa. The deities, heavily coated in ritual vermilion, are flanked by smaller attendant figures, including a seated Ganesha to the right, all resting upon a pedestal within a dimly lit, recessed sanctum typical of Newari temple architecture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This northern-side perspective captures the Pvāḥsyāḥ Phalcā, a traditional Newari public rest house, characterized by its low-profile, open-air structure built from brick and wood. The building features classic Newari craftsmanship, including intricate timber struts supporting the roof eaves and a traditional gajura pinnacle. In the foreground, a raised stone plinth enclosed by a chain fence displays various potted plants, contrasting with the surrounding urban environment of Nugaḥ Ṭola.
A phalcā in nepal.

This corner view of the Yanamugaḥ Kṛṣṇadyaḥ Phalcā in Yanāmugaḥ Ṭola reveals a traditional two-storey Newari public rest house, or phalcā, constructed from exposed red brick. The open ground floor, supported by sturdy brick pillars, serves as a communal space, while the upper level features a row of classic wooden-framed windows. Although the structure now carries a modern concrete slab roof, it retains its historic character amidst the dense urban fabric of the surrounding neighborhood.
A 2-storey phalcā in nepal.

A stone relief of a deity, likely Mahākāla, is enshrined within a recessed niche featuring a pointed, trefoil-arched frame characteristic of Newar architecture. The sculpture, heavily coated in layers of vermilion powder and ritual offerings, depicts the figure in a seated posture with hands held in a devotional gesture, set against a simple, unadorned stone backdrop.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche, depicts a multi-armed deity, likely Bhairava, heavily coated in ritual vermilion powder. The figure stands in a frontal pose atop a stylized lotus pedestal, framed by a pointed archway. Adorned with fresh floral offerings, the weathered sculpture exemplifies the enduring devotional practices central to Newari architectural spaces.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a stone staircase ascending toward a traditional *pati* or *mandapa* structure, which serves as a gateway to the historic Cilancva Caitya in Kirtipur. Beyond the timber-framed pavilion, the ancient Buddhist monument reveals its tiered base and iconic *gajura* pinnacle against the sky. The surrounding courtyard is framed by red brick Newari residences, with lush potted greenery flanking the stairs, emphasizing the integration of sacred heritage within the domestic urban fabric.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This ancient, weathered stone vessel, likely a ritual basin or *jalādhāra* associated with the sacred Cilancva Caitya site, displays a robust, circular form with a prominent structural fissure. Resting upon a brick-paved courtyard, the thick, porous stone rim shows signs of age and erosion, containing loose debris within its hollowed, darkened interior, reflecting the enduring legacy of Newari devotional architecture.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone relief of the Buddha Amitābha sits in meditative dhyāna mudrā, framed by an intricate, arching toraṇa featuring stylized floral motifs and a central makara-topped crown. Adorned with a vibrant red cloth offering and traces of ritual pigments, this devotional sculpture is set against a moss-covered brick wall, reflecting the enduring spiritual legacy of the site’s ancient architectural tradition.
A stone shrine,statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal western view captures the historic Licchavi-period Nātheśvara Śivaliṅga, a cylindrical stone phallus resting upon a circular yoni-pitha base inscribed with ancient script. Situated amidst the sacred Pashupatinath complex, the shrine is surrounded by weathered stone paving, a low white-plastered boundary wall, and traditional brick structures, including a nearby shikhara-style temple, reflecting the enduring architectural legacy and spiritual continuity of the Kathmandu Valley’s ancient Shaiva heritage.
A shaiva shrine,archaeological object(s) in nepal.

This weathered stone śivaliṅga, dating to the Licchavi period, stands prominently upon a circular yoni-pitha within a temple courtyard. Its cylindrical shaft features a distinct, textured middle band and a smooth, rounded top, while the surrounding environment includes historic stone structures adorned with intricate relief panels and arched tympanums, reflecting the enduring legacy of classical Newari craftsmanship.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Poḍe Pāṭī, a traditional single-story *phalcā* characterized by its distinctive gable roof and sturdy brick masonry. The open-fronted structure is supported by elegantly carved wooden pillars and features intricate decorative struts, typical of Newari architectural craftsmanship. Situated near the path to the Ārya-ghāṭa, this historic public rest house serves as a communal space where people gather, reflecting its enduring role in the social and cultural life of the heritage site.
A 1-storey phalcā in nepal.

This frontal view of the Thahiti Bhagavati Mandira showcases a traditional Newari doorway framed by intricately carved dark timber, featuring a prominent, polychrome wooden torana above the entrance depicting a central deity. The vibrant red brick masonry contrasts with the dark, ornate woodwork, while the open threshold reveals a glimpse into the temple’s inner sanctum, where a devotee engages in ritual worship within the sacred, light-filled space of this historic Shakta shrine.
A 1-storey shakta tiered temple in nepal.

A weathered brass statue of a recumbent tiger, the traditional *vāhana* (mount) of the goddess Bhagavatī, rests upon a rectangular pedestal inscribed with Devanagari script. Positioned within a temple courtyard, the figure displays stylized facial features and remnants of ritual offerings, serving as a devotional guardian that reflects the enduring influence of Newari metalworking traditions in religious architecture.
A brass statue at a nepali heritage site.

Suspended within a Newari temple courtyard, this weathered brass bell features a robust, hand-forged iron yoke adorned with stylized floral motifs and integrated chains. The bell’s crown is embellished with remnants of ritual offerings, including dried marigold petals and sacred thread, reflecting its active devotional use within the sanctified space of this eighteenth-century Shakta shrine.
A brass bell at a nepali heritage site.

This western-side view captures a weathered brick ḍabalī platform, a traditional raised stage used for public gatherings and ritual performances, situated before a multi-storied Newari structure undergoing extensive restoration. The site is enclosed by industrial scaffolding, partially obscuring the building’s traditional masonry and timber framework, while a small, weathered stone stupa and carved guardian lion sculptures remain visible atop the elevated, tiered base amidst the dry, overgrown courtyard.
A platform in nepal.

Captured from a northeastern angle, this compact Gaṇeśa Mandira features a single, gold-coated copper tiered roof supported by intricately carved wooden struts, reflecting the traditional Newari pagoda style. The structure, built of brick and stone, stands prominently at a crossroads, showcasing a stone-carved frame housing a hanging bell, while a gilded pinnacle or gajura crowns the apex, contrasting with the adjacent modern building and a vibrant blue door.
A 2-storey tiered temple in nepal.

A vibrant, vermilion-smeared stone image of Gaṇeśa sits enshrined within a recessed niche, framed by an ornate, gilded copper *toraṇa* featuring intricate floral motifs and celestial figures. Above this, a larger, triangular tympanum displays a central relief panel depicting a deity flanked by *caityas*, all set against the austere, darkened stone walls of the temple’s interior sanctuary.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Suspended within a rugged, dark stone frame, an ornate iron bell is encased in a protective cage of vertical metal bars. The surrounding wall features intricate golden-hued relief panels adorned with floral motifs, while the upper corners are marked by stylized stone lion heads, reflecting the refined craftsmanship typical of Newari architectural traditions.
A iron bell at a nepali heritage site.

This northeastern view captures the ongoing reconstruction of the 19th-century Nāga Bāhāḥ Phalcā, a traditional public rest house, where workers prepare the foundation amidst exposed brick masonry and steel rebar. The site’s transformation highlights the integration of modern construction materials with the original architectural footprint, while the adjacent golden-roofed pagoda structure, featuring a prominent *gajura* pinnacle, serves as a cultural anchor within this historic Newari courtyard setting.
A 2-storey phalcā in nepal.

A dark, stone-carved niche shrine featuring a central relief of the elephant-headed deity Gaṇeśa is set against a weathered red-brick wall. The shrine is crowned with a miniature, tiered roof structure topped by a golden finial, flanked by two stone guardian lions, and positioned beneath a traditional Newari wooden lattice window, reflecting the architectural vernacular of a restored urban courtyard.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

A vibrant, elongated mural of a red, scale-patterned serpent—a protective Nāga—undulates across the light blue interior wall of a traditional Newari phalcā. Below the serpentine body, stylized eyes and a triangular nose motif, reminiscent of a śivaliṅga or abstract deity representation, are painted directly onto the plaster, echoing the sacred iconography found within this recently reconstructed 19th-century pavilion.
A painting at a nepali heritage site.

This northwestern-facing view captures two traditional Newari *phalcā* (public rest houses) in Saugala Ṭola, characterized by their single-story, rectangular forms and distinctive gablet roofs adorned with weathered tiles and red valances. These open-sided structures, featuring wooden lattice screens and brick masonry, serve as vital communal spaces. Set against a backdrop of modern brick buildings and tangled power lines, the *phalcā* stand as enduring examples of vernacular urban heritage.
A 1-storey hindu phalcā in nepal.

This frontal view of the southern facade of the Bhelāchẽ Capāḥ reveals a traditional two-storey structure built from weathered, exposed brickwork, now showing significant signs of structural decay and debris. Affixed to the rough masonry is a vibrant, multi-armed deity painting framed by smaller ritual icons, contrasting sharply with the surrounding architectural ruin, while the building’s historic five-bayed layout and utilitarian design reflect its long-standing role as a communal Capāḥ for local caste groups.
A 2-storey hindu-buddhist,buddhist capāḥ in nepal.

This frontal view captures the modest, rectangular Dolakhādevī Mandira in Nuwakot, a single-story Shakta shrine constructed from red-painted brick. The structure features a simple hip roof topped with a metallic gajura, while the central entrance is framed by intricately carved wooden doorframes. Set within a paved courtyard, the temple’s austere brickwork and traditional craftsmanship reflect a functional, vernacular approach to religious architecture, standing in contrast to the surrounding residential buildings.
A 1-storey shakta shrine in nepal.

A weathered stone guardian lion, or *simha*, sits in a rigid, frontal pose against a rustic red brick wall at the Dolakhādevī shrine. The sculpture displays stylized, muscular anatomy and traditional decorative collars, with traces of red vermilion powder, or *sindur*, marking its head and paws, highlighting its role as a consecrated protector within this sacred Newari space.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered brass bell hangs suspended by a twisted metal chain, its dark, patinated surface accented with traces of ritual vermilion powder. The bell features a traditional flared profile and a simple clapper ring at its base, reflecting its devotional function within the modest, rustic setting of the Dolakhādevī shrine, where it serves as a tactile instrument for worshippers.
A brass bell at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view of the Triśūlī Gaṅeśa shrine in Triśūlībajāra reveals a modest, single-story structure characterized by a simple hip roof covered in corrugated metal sheets. The orange-plastered masonry walls are protected by a decorative red valance, while the shrine is crowned by a modest golden pinnacle or gajura. Enclosed by a metal gate and a blue-painted fence, the site integrates traditional religious elements with contemporary urban surroundings, including nearby market goods.
A 1-storey bell-shaped (temple/shrine) in nepal.

This brass statue of a shrew, the traditional vehicle (vāhana) of Lord Gaṇeśa, sits within a tiled devotional niche. The metallic figure is depicted in a seated posture, holding a small offering in its raised paw, and is adorned with a beaded collar, showing signs of ritual wear from vermilion powder applied during active worship.
A brass statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered brass temple bell, suspended by iron rings within a metal-grille framework, features intricate relief inscriptions and a traditional floral frieze along its upper rim. Traces of vermilion powder, a hallmark of daily ritual devotion, cling to its oxidized surface, while the central clapper hangs poised within the resonant, aged vessel, reflecting the enduring spiritual atmosphere of this sacred site.
A brass bell at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Candrabhāneśvara Mandira, a modest, single-storey Shaiva shrine characterized by a distinct bell-shaped dome roof topped with a metal-clad pinnacle, or gajura. The yellow-plastered structure features a central arched entrance protected by a metal grille, while a stone Nandi statue and a ritual bell stand on the foreground plinth, set amidst the contrasting textures of weathered brick masonry and modern residential buildings in the surrounding courtyard.
A 1-storey shaiva bell-shaped (temple/shrine) in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of the deity Vishnu, depicted in a standing posture with multiple arms, is enshrined within a recessed, rustic niche. The icon, heavily coated in layers of ritual vermilion powder, displays traditional iconography characteristic of Newari devotional art, resting above a small, darkened clay lamp used for daily offerings within this humble sanctuary.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone śivaliṅga, housed within a modest sanctum, features a central phallic emblem resting upon a circular yoni pedestal with a prominent spout. The base is surrounded by several rounded votive stones, reflecting a traditional Shaiva devotional arrangement, while the worn texture and scattered vermilion offerings indicate its ongoing ritual significance within the temple’s austere interior setting.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ajayamerukoṭa temple group 1, śikhara temples with pavilion 1 (built around 1373 CE)
captions: This frontal view captures the 14th-century Ajayamerukoṭa temple complex, featuring two stone śikhara structures flanking a central pavilion currently undergoing restoration. Each temple displays a truncated, pyramidal stone body topped with a distinct circular āmalaka pinnacle, while the surrounding scaffolding highlights ongoing conservation efforts. Nestled within a lush, overgrown landscape, these Shaiva shrines exemplify the austere, robust masonry characteristic of medieval Western Nepali temple architecture.
A 1-storey shaiva,hindu śikhara temple,pavilion in nepal.

monument_name: Ajayamerukoṭa temple group 1, śikhara temples with pavilion 1 (built around 1373 CE)
captions: A young man rests within the stone pavilion of a 14th-century temple complex, characterized by heavy, weathered masonry and intricate relief carvings on its structural pillars. The open-air pavilion, currently supported by modern metal scaffolding, features a prominent circular stone element in the background, reflecting the austere architectural style of this historic Shaiva site.
A stone object at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Ajayamerukoṭa temple group 1, śikhara temples with pavilion 1 (built around 1373 CE)
captions: This stone śikhara temple, currently undergoing restoration, features a tiered, tapering tower adorned with a central relief panel and a delicate stone-carved tympanum. The structure’s masonry is marked with alphanumeric labels for reconstruction, while its base displays weathered iconographic carvings, all set against a rugged, grassy hillside and supported by a temporary metal scaffolding structure.
A stone shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a frontal perspective, this stone-built Tukunā Hiti serves as a traditional public water spout along the roadside in Sera. The structure features a central arched facade adorned with faded fish motifs and a prominent *toraṇa*-like crest inscribed with Nepali script. Water flows from a metal pipe into a basin beneath a small, recessed stone carving of Bhagīratha, set against a backdrop of weathered masonry, lush vegetation, and moss-covered stone steps.
A water architecture in nepal.

A weathered stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche of a moss-covered masonry wall, depicts a standing deity in anjali mudra. The figure, adorned with traces of vermilion and a fresh floral offering, exhibits the characteristic stylistic features of medieval Newari sculpture, with its softened facial features and rounded, devotional form emerging from the damp, ancient stonework.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone *hiti* (traditional water spout) features a central, weathered spout head projecting from a masonry wall, flanked by faint, hand-drawn fish motifs. Below, a small, moss-covered relief panel depicts a deity, likely a guardian figure, receiving the continuous flow of water, all set within a semi-circular tympanum inscribed with Nepali script commemorating its communal construction and restoration.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Bhairaveśvara Mahādeva shrine, a modest, single-story Shaiva temple characterized by its vibrant yellow-painted masonry walls and a simple hip roof. The entrance is adorned with a metallic trident, while a prominent Nandi bull sculpture sits in the foreground. Located within the Jālapādevī Pīṭha premises, the shrine features a modest pinnacle and is surrounded by a serene courtyard shaded by mature trees and traditional architectural elements.
A 1-storey shaiva shrine in nepal.

A weathered stone sculpture of Nandi, the sacred bull and vehicle of Shiva, rests in a devotional posture before a Shaiva shrine. The figure, coated in layers of ritual yellow and red vermilion powder, exhibits a worn, rounded form characteristic of long-term veneration, positioned prominently on a plinth near the base of an ancient tree.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone Bhairaveśvara śivaliṅga, housed within the sanctum, features a weathered, anthropomorphic relief panel draped in a vibrant saffron cloth and adorned with fresh hibiscus blossoms. Resting upon a substantial stone base, the deity is surrounded by ritual offerings, including coconuts and metal vessels, set against the rustic, earthen floor of the traditional Shaiva shrine.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the historic Lagana Hiti showcases a sunken water conduit constructed from traditional red brick masonry, featuring three intricately carved stone spouts shaped like makaras. Above the spouts, the wall is adorned with miniature shrines topped with stone pinnacles, while the surrounding environment includes weathered brick buildings, remnants of wooden debris, and a stone guardian lion, reflecting the enduring legacy of this ancient water infrastructure.
A buddhist,hindu water architecture in nepal.

A weathered, moss-covered stone relief of a deity, likely a youthful attendant or attendant figure, is set into a brick wall at a historic water conduit. The figure is depicted in a reverent posture with hands joined in an añjali mudrā, emerging from the dark, damp masonry where water has continuously flowed for centuries, reflecting the enduring sanctity of this ancient site.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This ancient stone *hiti* (water spout) features a meticulously carved *makara* (mythical sea creature) head from which water flows, set against a weathered brick wall adorned with ornate stone relief panels depicting deities. Above the spout, a miniature *caitya* and arched niches house intricate stone carvings, reflecting the enduring architectural legacy of the Licchavi period.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Nhū Bāhāḥ Nani Jinālaya Caitya (1916 CE)
captions: Captured from a frontal eastern perspective, this stone *caitya* at Nhū Bāhāḥ Nani stands as a sacred Buddhist monument featuring a multi-tiered base and a central lotus pedestal supporting four seated figures. The structure culminates in a traditional *gajura* pinnacle, while the surrounding environment includes a metal offering rail and the distinct red-brick masonry of Newari residential architecture, characterized by intricate wooden window carvings and traditional *pati*-style building facades.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

monument_name: Nhū Bāhāḥ Nani Jinālaya Caitya (1916 CE)
captions: This dark stone votive caitya features a central Akṣobhya Buddha seated in the bhūmisparśa mudrā, or earth-touching gesture, on a pedestal adorned with a delicate relief panel. The multi-faced sculpture is intricately carved with traditional Newari motifs, showing signs of ritual veneration through scattered marigold petals and traces of vermilion powder applied to the serene, stylized figures.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Nhū Bāhāḥ Nani Jinālaya Caitya (1916 CE)
captions: This dark stone caitya features a central four-faced image of the Buddha, with the southern façade displaying Ratnasambhava in the bhūmisparśa mudrā. The sculpture, adorned with ritual vermilion powder and flower offerings, sits upon a pedestal intricately carved with relief panels, showcasing the refined craftsmanship characteristic of Newari devotional art within a traditional courtyard setting.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Konti Ṭola Gaṇeśa Mandira reveals a modest, single-storey Hindu temple characterized by its traditional hip roof and ornate, gilded metalwork surrounding the central entrance. Flanked by two stone guardian lions, the structure features a prominent golden *toraṇa* above the doorway, while the roof is crowned by a metallic *gajura* pinnacle, all set within a paved courtyard alongside a metal lamp stand and modern urban surroundings.
A 1-storey hindu tiered temple in nepal.

A red-pigmented stone image of the multi-armed deity Gaṇeśa sits in a meditative posture within a recessed shrine. Above the entrance, an ornate, semi-circular brass toraṇa features a central relief of the deity flanked by mythical makaras and a fierce kīrtimukha mask, all set against a backdrop of white-tiled walls and flanking stone guardian figures.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone sculpture depicts Nandi, the sacred bull and vahana of Shiva, resting in a traditional recumbent posture on a rectangular plinth within a temple courtyard. The figure features a prominent hump and subtle anatomical modeling, with traces of vermilion pigment on its head, reflecting its ongoing role as a devotional object in Newari religious practice.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Cibāhāḥ Phalcā showcases a traditional Newari public rest house characterized by its modest, single-story rectangular form and a prominent gable roof densely covered in moss and vegetation. The structure features finely carved dark timber pillars supporting the eaves, set upon a raised stone plinth, while the interior brick walls house a wooden partition. Situated in a quiet urban courtyard, this historic monument exemplifies vernacular architectural endurance.
A buddhist,hindu phalcā in nepal.

This frontal view of the Dharmamukteśvara Mandira in Phirkyāpa reveals a modest, single-story Shaiva shrine constructed from light-colored brick masonry. The structure features a hip roof topped by a golden *gajura* pinnacle, while a small stone Nandi statue sits prominently on a pedestal before the central arched doorway. Set within a grassy, rural landscape, the temple is adorned with festive prayer flags and framed by lush trees and distant hills.
A 1-storey shaiva bell-shaped (temple/shrine) in nepal.

A weathered stone sculpture of the sacred bull Nandi rests atop a tiered, block-like plinth, its form characterized by a recumbent posture and a serene, upward-gazing expression. This devotional icon, displaying signs of age and lichen growth, stands in an open courtyard setting, serving as a traditional guardian figure oriented toward the nearby Shaiva shrine.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This ancient stone śivaliṅga, housed within a dimly lit sanctum, features a cylindrical phallic form resting upon a circular yoni-pīṭha base, which is intricately carved with a serpent motif. A metallic cobra hood rises behind the linga, accompanied by a small ritual bell, all set against a backdrop of a metal-grated window that reveals a sunlit courtyard beyond.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southwestern angle, the Jora Gaṇeśa Mandira stands as a classic three-tiered pagoda temple constructed from dark, intricately carved wood and brick. The structure features elaborate struts supporting its tiered roofs, which culminate in a metallic gajura pinnacle, while the ground level showcases a decorative toraṇa above the entrance, flanked by stone lion guardians and a resting dog, all nestled amidst the dense urban fabric of traditional Newari architecture.
A 3-storey hindu-buddhist tiered temple in nepal.

A weathered stone sculpture of a recumbent mouse, the traditional *vāhana* (mount) of Lord Gaṇeśa, rests within a protective iron enclosure. The figure is heavily coated in vermilion powder and adorned with scattered floral offerings, its form resting upon a stylized, petal-carved base that reflects the devotional practices common to the historic Newari urban landscape.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Framed by two stone guardian lions, this frontal view captures the entrance to the Mikhā Bāhāḥ in Lalitpur, where a modest yellow-painted metal structure houses a small, ornate votive stupa. The central shrine, positioned within the monastic courtyard, features a tiered base and a metallic pinnacle, reflecting the humble yet sacred character of this Buddhist site, which serves as a significant branch of the historic Hiraṇyavarṇa Mahāvihāra.
A buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone image of the Buddha Akṣobhya, enshrined within a Newari monastic sanctum, features a serene countenance adorned with a metallic crown and a floral offering. The central figure is flanked by smaller relief panels on a tiered pedestal, all set against a white-washed backdrop framed by iron lamp-stands, reflecting the devotional atmosphere of a historic Buddhist *bāhāḥ*.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya, situated within a traditional Newari courtyard, features a tiered base adorned with a central relief panel depicting a meditative figure. The structure is crowned by a classic stupa form, while a small, ornate toraṇa frames a miniature deity, all set within a yellow metal frame decorated with hanging prayer wheels and potted plants.
A stone vajradhātu caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Viśveśvara Mandira in Patan Durbar Square showcases a classic Newari tiered temple constructed from brick and intricately carved wood. The elevated platform features stone guardian lions flanking the central staircase, while the structure above displays elaborate wooden struts supporting the eaves. The temple’s architectural elegance is defined by its ornate craftsmanship, reflecting the profound Shaiva traditions and artistic mastery characteristic of 17th-century Kathmandu Valley temple design.
A 3-storey shaiva tiered temple in nepal.

A stone sculpture of Nandi, the sacred bull of Shiva, rests in a recumbent posture upon a tiered brick plinth. Carved with stylized musculature and a prominent hump, the weathered figure faces towards the temple, serving as a traditional vahana (mount) positioned in front of the sanctum, while a guardian lion statue stands on a separate pedestal nearby.
A statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone śivaliṅga, marked with a vibrant red and yellow painted Om symbol, rests upon a multi-tiered, square pedestal within a brick-walled sanctum. The cylindrical phallic icon rises from a circular yoni base, which features a prominent drainage spout extending to the right, reflecting the traditional architectural devotion found in Newari Shaiva shrines.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

A frontal view captures the dark stone image of Akṣobhya Buddha seated in the bhūmisparśa mudrā within the dyaḥchẽ of Puṇyacandra Bāhāḥ. The deity rests upon a lotus pedestal atop a carved stone plinth, framed by a prominent prabhāmaṇḍala or halo featuring stylized flame motifs. This sacred icon, marked with a vermilion tilaka, is surrounded by modest ritual offerings placed upon the stone altar within the dimly lit, austere monastic interior.
A 1-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This stone Dharmadhātu maṇḍala, situated within a Newari monastic courtyard, features a central octagonal base adorned with intricate relief panels depicting deities framed by arched niches. The structure is capped by a stylized, multi-petaled lotus pedestal, with the upper register bearing a finely inscribed Sanskrit band, reflecting the devotional craftsmanship characteristic of traditional Buddhist architecture in the Kathmandu Valley.
A stone dharmadhātu at a nepali heritage site.

This southeastern perspective captures the Seto Gaṇeśa Guṭhī Capāḥchẽ, a traditional four-storey Newari structure defined by its exposed red brick masonry and intricate dark-timber woodwork. The ground level features a series of open-arched bays enclosed by metal grilles, showcasing the building's function as a community *capāḥ*. Above, the façade displays traditional latticed windows and ornate carved struts, reflecting the classic craftsmanship synonymous with the historic architecture of Om Bāhāḥ, Lalitpur.
A 4-storey vaishnava capāḥ in nepal.

This stone relief panel, set within a recessed wall niche, depicts the Vaishnava triad of Jagannātha flanked by Balarāma and Subhadrā. Each standing deity is framed by a distinct, ornate *toraṇa*-like arch, while the central figure of Subhadrā is rendered in a smaller scale with hands joined in *añjali mudrā*, all resting upon a stylized lotus pedestal.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel, housed within a niche, depicts the divine couple Lakṣmī-Nārāyaṇa standing in a graceful tribhanga posture upon a lotus pedestal. The central deities are flanked by a small kneeling devotee and a recumbent mount, all framed by a simple arched tympanum, with traces of red and yellow pigment still visible on their faces.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This southwestern corner view captures the ancient Lo Gekar monastery, a significant 8th-century Buddhist monastic structure characterized by its distinct two-storey, flat-roofed form. The exterior walls are finished in contrasting red and white lime plaster, while a long, low wall adorned with rows of prayer wheels lines the perimeter. The site is further defined by a rugged stone foundation, scattered prayer flags, and a prominent *gajura* pinnacle crowning the roof.
A 2-storey buddhist monastic building (bāhāḥ) in nepal.

This central clay statue of Padmasambhava, characterized by his distinctive hat, mustache, and ritual implements, sits within a shrine adorned with intricate floral metalwork and a backdrop of mural-painted deities. The figure is draped in traditional ceremonial scarves, set against a weathered wall featuring rows of small, painted relief panels that reflect the ancient, spiritually charged atmosphere of this historic Himalayan monastic sanctuary.
A terracotta statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a direct eastern perspective, this three-tiered pagoda-style Buddhist shrine is constructed from red brick and features a traditional hip roof adorned with a central metallic gajura pinnacle. The structure is enclosed by a metal fence and features intricate wooden struts supporting the eaves, while the entrance is framed by a decorative toraṇa, reflecting the rich architectural heritage of the region where the Mucalinda Buddha deity is enshrined.
A 3-storey buddhist shrine in nepal.

This stone relief depicts the Buddha in a meditative posture, sheltered by the multi-headed hood of the serpent king Mucalinda, whose coils form the base of the throne. Situated within a modest sanctum, the weathered sculpture is adorned with ritual offerings, vibrant vermilion powder, and scattered flower petals, highlighting its continued role as a focal point for active devotional worship.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view of the Yanamugaḥ Capāḥ highlights the traditional Newari craftsmanship of this two-storey public rest house, characterized by its exposed red brick masonry and intricate wooden structural elements. The prominent corner post supports a deep, overhanging upper balcony featuring delicate wooden railings, while the ground floor opens into a sheltered space, serving as a vital *pati* for social gatherings within the historic Yanāmugaḥ Ṭola neighborhood.
A 2-storey capāḥ in nepal.

This stone relief panel, embedded within a weathered brick wall, depicts the divine couple Lakṣmīnārāyaṇa seated in lalitāsana upon a lotus pedestal. The figures, heavily coated in vermilion powder and adorned with fresh floral offerings, exhibit classic Newari iconographic features, with the deity holding his characteristic attributes while embracing his consort in an intimate, devotional display of sacred union.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This Nāsaḥdyaḥ shrine, located within a traditional Newari *capāḥ*, features two hand-painted cloth *paubhā*-style hangings depicting ritual iconography, including deities, animals, and auspicious symbols. These textiles are suspended against a recessed wooden wall panel above a simple altar, flanked by a framed mirror and a rustic wooden stand, reflecting the modest yet sacred interior of this communal cultural space.
A cloth shrine at a nepali heritage site.

This northwestern corner view captures the Lāchi Gaṇeśa Mandira, a distinctive Hindu shrine featuring a white-plastered square base topped by a smooth, hemispherical dome and a gilded *gajura* pinnacle. Adjacent to this domed structure stands a larger, open-sided *mandapa* or shrine enclosure protected by yellow metal grilles, while the surrounding paved courtyard is framed by modern concrete buildings and a prominent storefront featuring a Pepsi-branded shutter.
A 1-storey hindu domed temple in nepal.

A dark, weathered stone relief of the elephant-headed deity Gaṇeśa sits enshrined within a recessed alcove framed by a decorative arch. The deity is depicted in a seated posture, adorned with ritual offerings of flowers and vermilion, set against a backdrop of light-colored, grid-patterned tiles that contrast with the deep, textured patina of the ancient stone sculpture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A robust, dark stone lion, carved in a traditional Newari style, sits as a guardian figure upon the square stone plinth of a domed temple. The sculpture displays intricate mane detailing and a stylized, open-mouthed expression, with a small votive offering placed within its jaws, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices that animate these historic architectural settings.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Jānakī Mandira showcases a grand, multi-storied structure defined by its distinctive Mughal-influenced domes and intricate masonry, standing as a masterpiece of Neo-Rajput architecture. The facade features a symmetrical arrangement of arched windows and ornate parapets, while the central entrance is adorned with vibrant floral decorations, leading into a courtyard bustling with devotees beneath the temple’s towering, white-washed pinnacles and golden gajura that pierce the clear sky.
A domed temple,courtyard building in nepal.

This northeastern side view captures the red-brick masonry of the Manamāneśvarī shrine, a modest, single-storey *dyaḥchẽ* characterized by its traditional gable roof and simple, functional aesthetic. The wall features a prominent Om symbol, a small wooden window with a lattice screen, and a row of ritual oil lamps mounted on a metal railing, while a vibrant rose bush frames the stone-paved path leading toward the entrance.
A 1-storey shakta esoteric shrine (dyaḥchẽ, āgãchẽ) in nepal.

A vibrant red curtain conceals the sacred stone image of Manamāneśvarī within this modest *āgãchẽ*, flanked by weathered wall paintings of Hindu deities. The rugged, unadorned masonry and exposed timber ceiling underscore the antiquity of this esoteric shrine, which preserves a deep historical connection to the ancient cult of Māneśvara dating back to the seventh century.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

An ancient, weathered stone śivaliṅga, adorned with vibrant, hand-painted orange and red floral motifs, stands as the central focus within this sacred inner sanctum. Enclosed by a metal railing, the icon is surmounted by a ritual copper vessel and a symbolic trident, while a hanging bell and nearby votive offerings reflect the enduring, living devotional traditions of this historic site.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

This southeastern corner view captures a row of ancient, low-profile brick caityas situated along the Pā̃camāne ridge, characterized by their cylindrical, unadorned masonry drums resting on simple concrete plinths. These Buddhist monuments lack traditional architectural elements like the harmikā or gajura, instead serving as naturalized shrines adorned with weathered prayer flags that flutter amidst the dense foliage of the surrounding hillside, emphasizing their role as humble, landscape-integrated votive structures.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This dark stone sculpture depicts the Buddha in a meditative posture, sheltered by the protective hood of a multi-headed nāga. Seated upon a lotus-petal pedestal, the figure displays the abhaya mudra with his right hand while holding a small offering vessel, all set within a serene, wooded landscape near the historic Pā̃camāne votive caityas.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This side-view detail of the Devīghāṭa Sattala (1815 CE) highlights the weathered, heavy timber construction characteristic of this historic two-storey *sataḥ* (public rest house). The image focuses on the robust, hand-hewn wooden beams and vertical posts that support the structure’s gable roof, contrasting the aged, dark-stained timber with the surrounding corrugated metal siding and the simple, utilitarian courtyard of this significant pilgrimage site at the confluence of the Triśulī and Tādī rivers.
A 2-storey sataḥ in nepal.

This frontal view of the Baṭārabajāra Bhīmasena shrine showcases a compact, brick-built structure topped with a white-washed dome and a golden *gajura* pinnacle. The central relief of the deity is framed by a stone *toraṇa* arch, flanked by suspended ritual bells. Set within a bustling Newar settlement, this modest shrine reflects the traditional practice of establishing Bhīmasena, the patron deity of merchants, at the heart of daily community life.
A hindu shrine in nepal.

This stone relief panel depicts the deity Bhīmasena, characterized by his robust physique, mustache, and sword, set within a recessed niche framed by a weathered brick wall. The figure is crowned by a finely carved *toraṇa* featuring a fierce *kīrtimukha* mask, while the entire sculpture is heavily layered with vermilion powder and ritual offerings typical of active Newar devotional practice.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered, cast-brass ritual bell hangs suspended by a heavy iron chain, its surface marked by the patina of age and remnants of vermilion powder used in devotional offerings. Intricate Devanagari inscriptions are embossed along the lower rim of the bell, which serves as a traditional sonic instrument for invoking the deity within this prominent Newar merchant-community shrine.
A brass bell at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a frontal perspective, this stone water spout structure in Sāmarī village features a weathered, arched niche housing a central relief deity, adorned with a small hanging brass bell and a vibrant pink flower. The masonry, showing signs of age and lichen, stands amidst corrugated metal surroundings, while a modern tile depicting Hanuman and a smaller devotional plaque are placed prominently in the foreground, grounding this historic site within its contemporary rural context.
A water architecture in nepal.

A weathered, dark stone relief panel, likely depicting a Hindu deity, is enshrined within a simple arched niche at Sāmarī Dhārā. The heavily eroded sculpture, accented with ritualistic splashes of vermilion and yellow pigment, sits beneath a suspended brass bell, while a single vibrant flower rests atop the stone, marking this site as a place of ongoing local devotion.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone water spout, or *hiti*, features the stylized, zoomorphic head of a *makara*—a mythical aquatic creature—protruding from a masonry wall. While the intricate relief carving of the creature’s snout and scales shows signs of age and erosion, a modern brass faucet has been inserted into its mouth, blending traditional Newari craftsmanship with contemporary utilitarian function.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the two-storey Candanabharāteśvara Mandira showcases a classic Newari pagoda-style temple, characterized by its tiered, golden-tiled roofs and a prominent central *gajura* pinnacle. The structure features red brick masonry, intricate wooden latticed windows, and a central doorway, while the eaves are supported by traditional carved wooden struts. The paved courtyard includes smaller shrines and a metal railing, reflecting the temple's enduring significance as a Shaiva site of worship.
A 2-storey shaiva tiered temple in nepal.

A weathered, stone-carved Nandi bull, the traditional vehicle of Shiva, rests in a devotional posture at the Candanabharāteśvara temple complex. Its rough, lichen-spotted surface is adorned with ritual offerings of red powder and flower petals, highlighting its enduring role as a guardian figure positioned near the sanctum to honor the ancient Śivaliṅga housed within the tiered temple structure.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone shrine features an ornate, weathered toraṇa crowning a rectangular relief panel inscribed with Devanagari script. The arch is adorned with intricate carvings of seated deities, likely representing a Buddhist or Hindu pantheon, flanked by mythical guardians. Set against a vibrant yellow wall, the weathered sculpture exhibits traces of ritual vermilion offerings, highlighting its ongoing role as a devotional site.
A stone shrine at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a northeastern perspective, the octagonal Kṛṣṇa Cyāsĩdegaḥ stands as a magnificent three-storey stone Śikhara temple, distinguished by its intricate colonnaded galleries and tiered balconies. Its soaring, ribbed dome culminates in a traditional gilded gajura pinnacle, while the base is guarded by stone lions. This architectural masterpiece, set amidst the bustling brick-paved Patan Durbar Square, showcases the refined craftsmanship of Newari stonemasons through its rhythmic arches and delicate relief carvings.
A 3-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

This frontal view captures the 17th-century Cīthū Vajradhātu Caitya in Kirtipur, a classic stone stūpa structure characterized by its tiered base, recessed niches for Dhyānī Buddhas, and a crowning golden *gajura* pinnacle. Situated within a traditional Newari residential courtyard, the white-washed monument stands in stark contrast to the surrounding brick houses, while a local resident cleans the adjacent ground, highlighting the integration of sacred heritage into daily life.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This weathered stone relief panel, set into the plinth of a Buddhist caitya, depicts the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī seated in meditation alongside his consort. Both figures are framed by ornate, pointed aureoles within a shallow niche, their features softened by centuries of ritual application of vermilion powder, reflecting the enduring devotional practices associated with this sacred Newari site.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief, carved into a recessed niche, depicts the Buddha Ratnasambhava in the *varadamudrā* gesture of supreme generosity. The figure sits atop a lotus pedestal, his form softened by centuries of ritual application of vermilion and offerings, set within the sacred precinct of a historic Newar Buddhist *bahī* in Kirtipur.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A polished brass ritual bell hangs suspended from a dark, carved wooden toraṇa, its surface adorned with intricate floral bands and a weathered inscription along the rim. Secured by heavy iron chains and a makeshift wire loop, the bell serves as a devotional centerpiece, framed against a backdrop of traditional red brick masonry and lush greenery within the temple courtyard.
A brass bell at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Kṛṣṇa Mandira at Vala Ṭol (1725 CE)
captions: This western-facing view captures the 1725 CE Kṛṣṇa Mandira at Vala Ṭol, a sophisticated two-storey Vaishnava temple built in the stone śikhara style. The structure rests upon a three-plinth base featuring twelve columned porches, topped by a traditional tiered roof and crowned with a decorative pinnacle. It stands in a bustling urban courtyard, surrounded by contemporary buildings, utility wires, and smaller brick shrines adorned with colorful prayer flags.
A 2-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

monument_name: Kṛṣṇa Mandira at Vala Ṭol (1725 CE)
captions: This modest brick shrine, situated near the Kṛṣṇa Mandira in Vala Ṭol, features a recessed sanctum housing a stone image of Gaṇeśa adorned with vermilion. The structure is capped by a simple tiered roof and a miniature finial, while the lintel displays a weathered relief inscription, all framed by decorative brickwork and festive prayer flags strung across the facade.
A brick statue,shrine at a nepali heritage site.

monument_name: Kṛṣṇa Mandira at Vala Ṭol (1725 CE)
captions: This stone relief panel, set within a recessed brick niche, depicts the divine couple Umā-Maheśvara seated in lalitāsana upon a lotus pedestal. The deity Śiva, adorned with a trident, embraces his consort Pārvatī, while his mount, the bull Nandin, rests below. Weathered by time and marked with ritual vermilion, the sculpture exemplifies the refined iconography characteristic of Newari devotional art.
A stone shrine at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel depicts a multi-armed deity, likely Bhairava, carved in deep relief and heavily coated in layers of vermilion and ritual offerings. Set within a recessed brick niche, the weathered sculpture features a dynamic, fierce form adorned with scattered flower petals and green leaves, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices characteristic of Newari sacred spaces.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This semi-circular brick well, integrated into the base of a temple wall, features a weathered stone coping and a metal cover. The structure is adorned with a central relief panel depicting a stylized face, while a row of small, dark votive oil lamps rests along the horizontal wooden beam above, reflecting the site's ongoing devotional use.
A brick well at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a western perspective, this diminutive late medieval Vaishnava shrine features a stone-carved shikhara roof crowned by a traditional gajura pinnacle. The structure rests on a brick masonry plinth adorned with a central relief carving, while its arched entrance is framed by an intricate toraṇa. Beside the shrine stands a vibrant orange-painted pillar topped with a small umbrella, which shelters a figure of Garuda, the divine mount of Lord Nārāyaṇa.
A 1-storey vaishnava shrine in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of Garuḍanārāyaṇa, featuring the four-armed deity seated upon his mount, Garuda, is set against a rustic brick wall. The icon, marked by traces of ritual vermilion and yellow pigment, is adorned with fresh bougainvillea petals, reflecting its ongoing role as a focus of daily devotional practice within this late medieval Vaishnava shrine.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel, embedded within the masonry of a late medieval Vaishnava shrine, features four deities seated in meditative postures upon a lotus pedestal beneath a decorative *toraṇa* arch. The figures, including a depiction of Hanumān, are weathered and coated in ritual vermilion powder, highlighting the enduring devotional practices that continue to animate this ancient architectural fragment.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the austere, two-story vernacular structure of the Tinchuli Sanskrit Chhatrabas, characterized by its traditional timber-framed balcony and exposed wooden struts supporting the upper floor. The building features a minimalist aesthetic with plastered masonry walls and symmetrical doorways, contrasting with the modern presence of a parked motorcycle and a solitary student seated on a metal bench, reflecting the adaptive reuse of this historic site within the Kathmandu Valley.
A 41-storey vaishnava domed temple,bell-shaped (temple/shrine) in nepal.

A stone Garuḍa, the mythical avian mount of Lord Vishnu, kneels in a reverent *añjali mudrā* atop a lotus-petal capital crowning a weathered, rectangular monolithic pillar. Inscribed with the deity’s name in Devanagari, this *dhvajastambha* stands within a temple courtyard, its austere, moss-covered surface contrasting with the surrounding modern architecture and scattered construction materials.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

A heavy, weathered bronze devotional bell hangs suspended by iron chains beneath a stone archway, featuring a handle adorned with mythical makara-like figures flanking a central finial. The bell’s surface is intricately cast with horizontal bands of floral motifs and Sanskrit inscriptions, showing signs of oxidation and age, set against the backdrop of stone steps leading to a historic temple structure.
A object at a nepali heritage site.

This southeastern corner view captures a traditional Newari *sataḥ*, a public rest house characterized by its modest two-story brick masonry and open ground-floor *mandapa* supported by elegant wooden pillars. The structure features a simple tiled roof and exposed brickwork, reflecting the vernacular architectural style common in riverbank settlements, while the adjacent map illustrates its strategic placement along the waterfront, emphasizing the *sataḥ* as an essential communal space for travelers and local gatherings.
A sataḥ in nepal.

This frontal view captures the Purohitaghāṭa Sataḥ, a modest, traditional Newari public shelter characterized by its low-profile, single-story form and corrugated metal cladding. A prominent, weathered stone gajura (pinnacle) sits atop the structure, hinting at its sacred function. The space features an open-fronted design typical of a pati, with a makeshift shop occupying the interior, while a red motorcycle rests on the dusty foreground near stone steps.
A sataḥ in nepal.

This southeastern corner view of the Gutpau Gaṇeśa Phalcā highlights a finely carved wooden column supporting the structure’s overhanging eaves, showcasing intricate floral motifs and traditional Newari craftsmanship. Behind the protective metal grille, the dark stone image of Gaṇeśa, the revered area deity, rests within the brick-walled sanctum of this historic two-storey public rest house, which serves as a vital social and spiritual anchor for the local community.
A 2-storey phalcā in nepal.

A dark, stone relief of a standing Gaṇeśa, adorned with vibrant yellow marigolds, is enshrined within a deep, arched brick niche. The deity’s multi-armed form, characterized by a prominent belly and elephantine head, is carved in a classic Newari style, resting upon a lotus pedestal flanked by small, weathered attendants, reflecting the devotional sanctity of this local neighborhood shrine.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief, set within a recessed wall niche, features a circular, sun-like or lunar disc framed by stylized, radiating petals and bead-like ornamentation. Resting upon a lotus-petal pedestal, the aniconic carving is adorned with a fresh yellow chrysanthemum and scattered ritual rice grains, reflecting its ongoing role as a focal point for daily devotional offerings within the phalcā.
A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southeastern angle, this single-storey Shaiva temple at Ṭeku Dobhāna exemplifies the brick-built Śikhara style, featuring a curvilinear tower topped by a weathered pinnacle. The structure’s weathered masonry and singular entrance contrast with the adjacent modern pati, while a cart buried under dense brush rests at its base. The scene highlights the juxtaposition of traditional Newari architectural heritage against the encroaching urban landscape and power lines near the river confluence.
A 1-storey shaiva śikhara temple in nepal.

A weathered stone śivaliṅga, marked with yellow sectarian tripuṇḍra, rests upon a tiered yoni-pīṭha within a minimalist brick cella. The base features decorative relief panels of stylized lotus petals, reflecting traditional Newari craftsmanship, while the surrounding exposed brick walls and a small, recessed window emphasize the austere, meditative atmosphere of this sacred site at the river confluence.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a western perspective, this stone Umāmaheśvara Mandira exhibits the distinct North Indian-influenced Śikhara architectural style, characterized by its soaring, curvilinear tower and intricate masonry. The structure features a raised plinth, a colonnaded base with robust pillars, and ornate carvings, while its crowning pinnacle serves as a traditional gajura. Nestled within the dense urban fabric of Patan, the temple stands as a testament to 18th-century craftsmanship amidst surrounding residential buildings.
A 3-storey shaiva śikhara temple in nepal.

This intricately carved stone relief panel depicts the divine couple Umāmaheśvara, with Śiva seated in lalitāsana, his arm draped around Umā, who rests against his side.
