A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a northern perspective, this Dharmadhātujinālaya Caitya stands as a stone Buddhist monument characterized by its tiered base and a central structure adorned with multiple Buddha figures sheltered by a naga canopy. The caitya is crowned with a traditional pinnacle, or gajura, and is enclosed by a metal railing lined with numerous small ritual oil lamps, all set within a weathered courtyard featuring scattered offerings and brick paving.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

This frontal view of the eastern side of Mahākāla Phalcā highlights a singular wooden capital featuring a rare, small-scale erotic carving flanked by intricate, stylized makara-like mythical creatures. The weathered timber post, adorned with delicate floral motifs, supports a heavy wooden lintel beneath a red-painted brick wall, while modern metal grilles contrast with the traditional craftsmanship of this historic rest house, which is traditionally supported by sixteen ornate wooden struts.
A 1-storey phalcā in nepal.

Captured from a northeastern angle, the Svapu Hiti Phalcā stands as a modest, single-story brick masonry rest house characterized by three open, arched bays that reflect traditional Newari public architecture. Adjacent to the structure, the historic stone water spout, or hiti, features carved stone spouts that channel water into a paved courtyard, all set against a lush, forested hillside along the stone-stepped pilgrimage path leading toward the Vajrayoginī Mandira.
A 1-storey phalcā in nepal.

This northwestern corner view reveals the ruined remains of the Sālkhā Phalcā, a traditional Newari public rest house in Sankhu that collapsed during the 2015 earthquake. The structure’s base, constructed from exposed brick and heavy timber beams, now supports a chaotic mound of salvaged stones and debris, stripped of its original carved struts and tiered roof, standing as a somber testament to the loss of this community’s architectural heritage.
A phalcā in nepal.

This frontal view of the Taḥ Bāhāḥ Dhvākhā reveals a traditional Newari gate structure flanked by two-story red-brick buildings featuring classic timber-framed windows and tiled roofs. The white-plastered gateway is crowned with a decorative lintel and a small toraṇa-like relief, leading into the inner courtyard. The surrounding area includes a paved stone pathway, a low brick wall, and a rustic pati-style masonry structure, reflecting the enduring vernacular architecture of Kathmandu Valley.
A heritage monument in nepal.

This weathered stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche of the Taḥ Bāhāḥ gate, depicts a multi-armed deity seated in a meditative posture upon a lotus pedestal. The central figure features a prominent three-headed arrangement, with an additional head positioned above, while the surrounding hands hold various ritual attributes, all framed by an elegant, carved archway typical of Newari architectural ornamentation.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief panel, set within a recessed niche framed by a decorative arch, depicts a multi-armed deity in a meditative posture with hands joined in anjali mudra. The central figure, flanked by additional heads and seated atop a stylized lion, exhibits classic Newari iconographic features, showcasing the intricate craftsmanship typical of devotional architectural embellishments found in Kathmandu Valley.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This corner view captures the Taḥ Bāhāḥ Dabū, a traditional raised brick platform or *dabū* used for ritual performances, situated adjacent to a small, single-tiered pagoda-style shrine. The shrine features a red brick base, vibrant blue metal latticework, and a tiled roof adorned with a red valance, while the adjacent stone-paved platform remains open, framed by mature trees, a stack of salvaged bricks, and parked motorcycles in a quiet urban courtyard.
A platform in nepal.

Captured from the eastern side, this Vajradhātu Caitya features a tiered, square-based plinth supporting a white, hemispherical dome topped by a gilded harmikā and a multi-tiered gajura pinnacle. A small niche housing a seated Buddha figure adorns the base, while a metal frame protects the structure. Prayer flags stretch across the sky above, set against a backdrop of traditional red-brick Newari residential buildings and a lush courtyard.
A buddhist stūpa or caitya in nepal.

A weathered stone relief of Amitābha Buddha sits in meditative dhyāna mudrā within a recessed niche of a yellow-painted structure. The deity is framed by a pointed, ornate toraṇa featuring delicate floral motifs, while remnants of ritual offerings, including scattered rice and dried foliage, rest at the base of the pedestal, highlighting the statue’s ongoing role in active devotional practice.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone votive caitya features a tiered, multi-level base supporting a central drum adorned with relief panels and a lotus-petal pedestal. The structure is crowned by a weathered, multi-faced finial topped with a spire, flanked by two golden prayer wheels suspended from a yellow metal frame, all set within a courtyard enclosure that highlights the enduring tradition of Newari devotional architecture.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the entrance to a *Digi Chẽ*, a traditional Newari structure within a *bāhāḥ* characterized by its distinctive arched gateway supported by fluted, classical-style pillars. Above the arch, a red-painted deity figure stands prominently, while the white-washed facade features vibrant murals of a *mask* and a guardian figure, flanked by a brick-walled building on the left and a bustling street-side stall displaying colorful textiles on the right.
A 3-storey esoteric shrine (dyaḥchẽ, āgãchẽ) in nepal.

This high-relief stone sculpture, set within a recessed brick niche, depicts a wrathful deity adorned with a prominent flaming halo and intricate crown. The figure holds a ritual vessel and a mace, standing atop a prostrate form, while layers of vermillion powder and floral offerings indicate ongoing devotional worship within the sacred space of a traditional Newari Digi Chẽ.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone relief of the monkey deity Hanuman, set within a recessed niche at a Digi Chẽ, displays a weathered, anthropomorphic form heavily coated in ritual vermilion and scattered rice offerings. The figure, characterized by its robust, rounded modeling and characteristic mace, exemplifies the enduring devotional practices within Newari monastic courtyards, where such iconic sculptures serve as protective guardians.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view of the Mahādeva Hiti, a historic 1653 CE water architecture site, reveals a sunken stone-lined basin featuring a traditional carved stone spout (dhārā) that discharges water into a weathered bucket. The utilitarian concrete retaining walls, which replaced original masonry during the 2009 excavation, contrast with the surrounding overgrown hillside, while the damp, paved floor reflects the ongoing function of this restored heritage site within its contemporary landscape.
A water architecture in nepal.

This weathered stone relief panel, set within the masonry of the Mahādeva Hiti water conduit, depicts a four-armed female deity standing in a graceful tribhanga posture. The figure is framed by a faint, circular prabhamandala (halo) and exhibits worn features, with her lower right hand resting upon a spherical object, reflecting the stylistic characteristics of post-Licchavi or early Malla period iconography.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone water spout (hiti) features a meticulously carved makara, a mythical aquatic creature, whose open mouth serves as the conduit for water. The relief displays elaborate, swirling foliate motifs, while a seated human figure is carved below the spout, set against a weathered stone wall that contrasts with the modern plastic bottle placed atop the structure.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from an eastern perspective, this two-storey Taleju Kvātha Chẽ stands as a robust, brick-built Shakta fortress-shrine characterized by its functional gable roof and three distinct ground-floor entrances. The structure, which serves as both a residential god house and a defensive fort, sits elevated behind a red-brick perimeter wall, contrasting sharply with the surrounding modern concrete buildings and the traditional stone-lined well situated in the dusty, unpaved courtyard.
A 2-storey shakta residential building,shrine,fortress in nepal.

Captured from the eastern side, this image reveals the weathered facade of the historic Kvātha Chẽ, a two-storey residential structure dating to 1777 CE that once served as the home for the local dvāre. The building features traditional brick masonry and timber elements typical of Newari architecture, standing amidst a rustic courtyard where a green mat and a patterned rug lie spread across the paved ground, contrasting with the surrounding remnants of daily life.
A 2-storey residential building in nepal.

This eastern-facing view captures the Illātvāḥ Gaṇeśa Mandira, a traditional three-tiered pagoda temple constructed of red brick and timber. The structure features intricate wooden struts supporting its projecting roofs, which are adorned with a gilded pinnacle, or gajura. Decorated with vibrant prayer flags, the shrine stands as a focal point within the historic settlement, surrounded by contemporary buildings and a paved courtyard where local residents gather near the temple’s wooden lattice entrance.
A 3-storey hindu tiered temple in nepal.

This devotional relief panel, housed within a stone niche, features three standing figures carved in low relief, their forms heavily coated in thick layers of vermilion (sindur) and ritual offerings. Positioned before them are two rounded stone objects, likely a śivaliṅga and a smaller votive stone, all serving as focal points for daily worship within this traditional Newari shrine.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered stone relief, deeply coated in ritual vermilion paste and offerings, depicts a multi-armed deity, likely a form of Mahākālī or a fierce goddess, set within a recessed niche. The iconographic details are obscured by layers of pigment, yet the dynamic posture and remnants of traditional Newari sculptural forms remain evident against the surrounding red-painted brick wall.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a northwestern perspective, this weathered stone *dhunge dhara* (stone spout) at Kusi Dhvākhā Hiti features an intricately carved *makara* head from which water flows into a moss-covered basin. The structure is adorned with a relief of the deity Umāmaheśvara, set against a crumbling brick and stone wall embellished with traditional motifs, while delicate vegetation clings to the damp, aged masonry, highlighting the enduring craftsmanship of this historic Sankhu water conduit.
A water architecture in nepal.

A weathered stone relief panel depicting a standing figure of Bhagīratha, carved in a devotional posture with hands joined in añjalimudrā, is embedded within the damp, moss-covered masonry of the Kusi Dhvākhā Hiti. This eroded sculpture, set against a backdrop of brick and stone, exemplifies the integration of sacred iconography into the functional architecture of traditional Newari water conduits.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone water conduit, or *hiti*, protrudes from a moss-covered masonry wall, featuring intricate relief carvings of a stylized *makara*—a mythical aquatic creature—with a prominent snout and swirling foliate motifs. This ancient spout, showing signs of erosion and organic growth, serves as a functional and artistic element within the historic water infrastructure of the Sankhu region.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

This southern-facing perspective reveals the Tusinhe Hiti, a traditional sunken water conduit constructed from exposed red brick masonry. The structure features a recessed, rectangular bathing area centered around a stone water spout, flanked by tiered brick walls that reflect classic Newari hydraulic engineering. Surrounded by a landscape of scattered construction materials and urban development, the site remains a vital, albeit weathered, example of ancient water management architecture within the settlement.
A water architecture in nepal.

This weathered stone relief panel, embedded within the masonry of a traditional water conduit, depicts a corpulent Yakṣa figure carved in high relief. The deity is shown in a squatting posture with arms raised, serving as a protective guardian beneath the water spout, its features softened by centuries of exposure and the natural accumulation of moss and debris.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This stone water spout, or *hīti*, features a weathered, zoomorphic relief carving—likely a *makara*—projecting from a masonry wall. A modern metal pipe protrudes from the creature’s mouth, contrasting with the ancient, eroded stone texture. The spout is positioned above a relief panel, typical of the sophisticated hydraulic engineering and sculptural traditions found in Newar water architecture.
A stone hiti at a nepali heritage site.

This eastern-facing perspective of Patan Durbar Square showcases the exquisite stone masonry of the 17th-century Kṛṣṇa Degaḥ, a three-storey Vaishnava temple characterized by its soaring *śikhara* tower, ornate arched colonnades, and a crowning *gajura* pinnacle. Adjacent to this masterpiece, a traditional structure undergoes extensive restoration, its historic brickwork obscured by a dense web of bamboo scaffolding, while a stone pillar topped with a metallic Garuda stands sentinel over the bustling, pigeon-filled plaza.
A 3-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

This eastern-facing perspective captures the Mahādyaḥ Pukhū, a traditional water architecture site featuring a rectangular brick-walled pond enclosure. Centrally positioned behind the pond stands a modest, single-story *pati*—an open-sided public rest house—constructed with exposed brick and a sloped, tiled roof. The surrounding area, characterized by its utilitarian brick masonry and weathered surfaces, reflects the vernacular architectural heritage of the Newar community, serving as a communal space for local residents.
A water architecture in nepal.

This frontal view of the northern gate of Sīdhvākhā, a historic 13th-century fortress in Sankhu, showcases a robust arched entryway constructed from weathered red bricks. At the apex of the arch, a central brick pillar features a carved stone deity face nestled amidst wild vegetation, crowned by a traditional stone pinnacle resembling a *gajura*. The structure’s aged masonry and integrated sculptural elements exemplify the enduring, defensive architectural heritage of the Newar people.
A fortress,archaeological object(s) in nepal.

Embedded within a weathered brick wall, this stone relief panel depicts a multi-armed Bhagavatī standing in a graceful tribhanga posture. The deity is framed by an intricately carved stone toraṇa, featuring a central kīrtimukha mask flanked by mythical makaras, reflecting the traditional Newari architectural style that characterizes the historic gateway structures of Sankhu.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This weathered, aniconic stone deity, worshipped as a manifestation of Gaṇeśa, sits centrally against a rustic brick wall, heavily coated in ritual vermilion paste and scattered floral offerings. It is flanked by a pair of stout, stylized stone lions, their primitive features reflecting the archaic, weathered aesthetic characteristic of early medieval Newari devotional sculpture found within the historic gates of Sankhu.
A stone aniconic stone at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a frontal western perspective, this 18th-century stone Trivikrama Viṣṇu Mandira exhibits a compact, single-storey *śikhara* design characterized by three distinct tiers leading to a crowning *gajura* finial. The structure features a central arched doorway framed by an ornate *toraṇa*, while the weathered masonry sits amidst a debris-strewn urban landscape where local residents rest on the temple's stepped plinth, contrasting the ancient craftsmanship with the surrounding modern residential development.
A 1-storey vaishnava śikhara temple in nepal.

This finely carved stone relief panel depicts the Trivikrama form of Vishnu, captured in a dynamic stride with his right leg raised, brandishing a mace while holding a conch shell. Set within a flame-bordered *toraṇa* arch, the deity’s weathered, pigment-dusted figure stands against a dark, recessed background, illustrating the sophisticated iconographic traditions of Newari stone sculpture.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures a modest *dabū*, a traditional raised platform serving as a community shrine, featuring a corrugated metal roof supported by simple iron pillars. At its center, a green-painted masonry shrine houses an image of Sarasvatī, flanked by smaller deities. The structure is enclosed by a teal metal railing, while the brick base displays inscribed dedicatory plaques, reflecting its dual significance as a sacred space for both Hindu and Buddhist devotees.
A buddhist,hindu shrine in nepal.

This weathered stone relief, housed within a recessed niche, depicts the goddess Sarasvatī in a standing posture, characterized by her multi-armed form and serene countenance. The sculpture, heavily coated in ritual vermilion powder and floral offerings, exhibits classic Newari stylistic features, with the deity flanked by smaller attendant figures, all set against a dark, weathered stone backdrop typical of traditional devotional shrines.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered stone śivaliṅga, marked with vermilion, rests within a protective metal cage in a dusty courtyard. Beside it stands a tall, textured dhvajastambha topped with a prominent iron trident, or triśūla. This devotional arrangement, situated before a multi-story building, reflects the syncretic religious landscape where Hindu iconography is integrated into the daily life of the local community.
A stone liṅga at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a high-angle perspective, this weathered stone slab serves as a foundational element of the historic Dhũllātvāḥ Dabū in Sankhu. The rectangular block features three carved, lotus-like circular motifs, which are currently adorned with ritualistic vermillion, yellow powder, and scattered marigold petals. Set into the ground amidst the surrounding earth and stone paving, these decorative carvings highlight the sacred, functional nature of this traditional Newari public performance platform.
A platform in nepal.

Two weathered stone caityas stand on a platform in Sankhu, their surfaces partially coated in ritual vermilion powder. These votive stupas feature tiered bases, arched niches containing relief panels, and rounded domes topped with stylized finials. Positioned before a commercial storefront, these historic monuments represent the enduring integration of sacred Newari architecture within the contemporary urban landscape.
A stone caitya at a nepali heritage site.

This cylindrical stone well, weathered by time and usage, features a simple masonry structure topped by a segmented metal cover and a rope-bound bucket. It sits prominently on a patterned brick-paved courtyard, adjacent to a weathered stone caitya, reflecting the functional integration of water sources within the sacred and communal spaces of a traditional Newari settlement.
A stone well at a nepali heritage site.

Captured from a southeastern perspective, this modest Bhīmasena shrine features a simple, single-story masonry structure topped with a gilded *gajura* pinnacle. The frontal view reveals a recessed arched niche housing a vermilion-anointed deity, set against a backdrop of traditional brick residential architecture. Surrounded by a weathered iron fence, the shrine serves as a localized religious focal point, blending seamlessly into the dense urban fabric of the *ṭola* through its humble, functional design.
A hindu-buddhist shrine in nepal.

A stone relief panel depicts the deity Bhīmsena seated in a stylized posture, clutching a mace with his right hand while his left hand is raised in a gesture of protection. The figure is set within a recessed arched niche, adorned with vibrant vermilion powder and scattered marigold petals, reflecting the ongoing devotional practices of the local community.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

This frontal view captures the Dhuñllā Gaṇeśa shrine, a small, square structure characterized by a tiered, hip-roofed superstructure topped with a metallic pinnacle. The shrine is encased in modern white tiles featuring repetitive circular motifs, while the entrance is framed by a prominent, ornate brass toraṇa depicting traditional protective deities. Two weathered stone lions guard the base, and a simple broom leans against the tiled wall, highlighting the site's ongoing ritual maintenance.
A hindu shrine in nepal.

This weathered stone relief of Gaṇeśa, coated in layers of vermilion and floral offerings, sits within a dark, arched sanctum. The deity is depicted in a traditional seated posture, framed by a recessed stone niche that mimics a *toraṇa* arch, while small brass ritual bells hang from the ceiling, casting shadows over the worn, ancient sculptural form.
A stone statue at a nepali heritage site.

A weathered bronze ritual bell hangs from a rugged iron support bracket before a tiled shrine, its surface etched with Devanagari script and adorned with traditional floral motifs. Behind it, a gilded, ornate tympanum featuring intricate relief carvings frames the sacred space, where the presence of the deity is marked by the rich, vermilion-stained iconography characteristic of Newari devotional sites.
A other bell at a nepali heritage site.
